topics.title: Properties of Ultrasound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Ultrasound

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Ultrasound** is defined as a **mechanical, longitudinal, pressure wave** that propagates through a medium by means of cyclic compression and rarefaction of particles, operating at frequencies **above the audible limit of human hearing (>20 kHz)**. In diagnostic medical imaging, ultrasound typically operates in the frequency range of **2 MHz to 20 MHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagnostic Medical Ultrasound Spectrum:** The clinically used frequency range is **1 MHz – 40 MHz**, although the most common diagnostic band is **2 MHz – 15 MHz**. Therapeutic ultrasound (e.g., physiotherapy, HIFU) often uses lower frequencies of **0.5 MHz – 3 MHz**, while high-resolution intravascular imaging may extend up to **50 MHz**.

### 1.2 Physical Nature of the Wave

Ultrasound is fundamentally different from electromagnetic waves (like X-rays or visible light). It is a **mechanical wave**, meaning it **requires a physical medium** (solid, liquid, or gas) for propagation. It cannot travel through a vacuum.

| Property | Ultrasound (Mechanical) | X-Ray (Electromagnetic) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wave Type | **Longitudinal Pressure Wave** | Transverse Electromagnetic Wave |
| Medium Required | **Yes (mandatory)** | No (can travel in vacuum) |
| Velocity in Soft Tissue | **~1540 m/s** | ~3 × 10⁸ m/s (speed of light) |
| Source | Piezoelectric Crystal | X-Ray Tube / Synchrotron |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Slinky Spring & The Concert Crowd"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine pushing one end of a stretched Slinky spring forward and pulling it back repeatedly. The coils bunch up (compression) and spread apart (rarefaction), and this disturbance travels down the spring. Ultrasound behaves **identically**: the transducer (source) "pushes" tissue particles via rapid vibration, creating alternating regions of high pressure (compression) and low pressure (rarefaction). The wave energy moves forward, but the individual particles only **oscillate back-and-forth** in tiny arcs around their resting position — they do **not** travel with the wave.
>
> A second analogy: Think of a stadium crowd doing a "wave" — people stand up and sit down, but no individual person actually travels around the stadium. The energy (the wave) does.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants in Diagnostic Ultrasound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Speed of sound in soft tissue (c):** **1540 m/s** (standardized reference value used by all ultrasound scanners).
> - **Average density of soft tissue (ρ):** **1060 kg/m³**
> - **Characteristic acoustic impedance of soft tissue (Z):** **1.63 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)** (Rayl)
> - **Lower audible limit:** **20 kHz** (threshold above which sound is "ultrasonic")
> - **Upper diagnostic limit:** **~40–50 MHz** (beyond which tissue absorption becomes prohibitive)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Primary Properties of Ultrasound (KTU Core Focus)

The behaviour of ultrasound in biological tissue is governed by **five interdependent properties**. These are the **most frequently tested concepts** in KTU university examinations.

#### Property 1 — Propagation Speed (Velocity)

- The speed of ultrasound depends **only on the physical properties of the medium** (density and elasticity/stiffness), **not on the frequency or wavelength**.
- It is highest in **solids** (rigid molecular bonds), lower in **liquids**, and lowest in **gases** (loosely bound molecules).

> [!NOTE]
> **General Formula:**
>
> $$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}$$
>
> where **K** is the **bulk modulus** (elastic stiffness) of the medium and **ρ** is its **density**. Note that **c increases with stiffness** but **decreases with density**.

**Typical values in medical imaging:**

| Medium | Speed of Sound (m/s) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Air (lungs, GI tract) | **330** |
| Fat | **1450** |
| Water (CSF, vitreous humor) | **1480** |
| Soft Tissue (average reference) | **1540** |
| Liver | **1570** |
| Kidney | **1560** |
| Blood | **1570** |
| Muscle | **1580** |
| Bone (cortical) | **4080** |

#### Property 2 — Reflection and Acoustic Impedance

- Reflection occurs at the **boundary (interface) between two media** with different acoustic properties.
- The **amount of reflection** is determined by the difference in **characteristic acoustic impedance (Z)** of the two media.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acoustic Impedance Formula:**
>
> $$Z = \rho \cdot c$$
>
> where **ρ** = density (kg/m³) and **c** = speed of sound in the medium (m/s). The SI unit is the **Rayl** (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹).
>
> The **Reflection Coefficient (R)** at a perpendicular interface is:
>
> $$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

**Key engineering insight:** The **larger the impedance mismatch**, the **stronger the echo** returned to the transducer. This is why:
- **Soft tissue / soft tissue** interfaces (small Z mismatch) → small echoes → grayscale mid-tones.
- **Soft tissue / bone** or **soft tissue / air** interfaces (huge Z mismatch) → near-total reflection → bright white (hyperechoic) or acoustic shadow.

#### Property 3 — Refraction

- When ultrasound strikes an interface **at an oblique angle**, it **bends** (changes direction) as it crosses into the second medium.
- Governed by **Snell's Law**.

> $$ \frac{\sin \theta_1}{c_1} = \frac{\sin \theta_2}{c_2} $$

- Refraction can cause **misregistration artifacts** in imaging (the structure appears displaced from its true location).

#### Property 4 — Scattering

- When ultrasound encounters a **rough interface** or a **structure much smaller than its wavelength** (e.g., red blood cells, ~7 µm), the wave is **scattered in many directions**.
- Scattering is what allows the visualization of **parenchymal organs** (liver, kidney), which would otherwise appear as featureless black voids if only specular reflection were present.
- **Rayleigh scattering** dominates when particle size $\ll \lambda$, with intensity $\propto f^4$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rayleigh Scattering Intensity:**
>
> $$I_{scattered} \propto \frac{f^4}{r^2}$$
>
> This is why higher-frequency probes (e.g., 10 MHz) produce images with finer speckle texture and better resolution of microstructures — but at the cost of penetration depth.

#### Property 5 — Attenuation (Absorption + Scattering + Divergence)

- **Attenuation** is the **progressive loss of ultrasound intensity** as the wave travels through tissue. It is the **combined effect** of:
  1. **Absorption** (conversion of acoustic energy to heat — the dominant mechanism in soft tissue).
  2. **Scattering** (energy redirected away from the beam axis).
  3. **Beam divergence** (geometric spreading of the wavefront).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attenuation Coefficient (α):** Measured in **dB/cm/MHz**.
> - **Soft tissue (average):** 0.5 dB/cm/MHz
> - **Fat:** 0.6 dB/cm/MHz
> - **Liver:** 0.7 dB/cm/MHz
> - **Muscle:** 1.0 dB/cm/MHz
> - **Bone:** 5.0 dB/cm/MHz
> - **Air:** 12.0 dB/cm/MHz
>
> **Rule of thumb for soft tissue:** Total attenuation ≈ **1 dB/cm/MHz** (one-way).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning / Application | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $c = f \cdot \lambda$ | Fundamental wave relation; basis of all depth/time calculations | m/s, Hz, m |
| 2 | $c = \sqrt{K / \rho}$ | Speed of sound in a medium (depends on stiffness and density) | m/s |
| 3 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Characteristic acoustic impedance | Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹) |
| 4 | $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Intensity Reflection Coefficient at perpendicular interface | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | $T = 1 - R$ | Transmission Coefficient (intensity fraction) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\frac{\sin \theta_1}{c_1} = \frac{\sin \theta_2}{c_2}$ | Snell's Law for refraction | Degrees / m·s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $I_{attenuated} = I_0 \cdot e^{-2 \alpha x}$ | Exponential intensity decay (round trip) | W/m² |
| 8 | $\alpha_{soft\ tissue} \approx 1\ \mathrm{dB/cm/MHz}$ | Practical attenuation rule of thumb | dB·cm⁻¹·MHz⁻¹ |
| 9 | $I_{scattered} \propto f^4 / r^2$ | Rayleigh scattering (small particles) | W/m² |
| 10 | $\mathrm{Axial\ Resolution} = \frac{c}{2 f}$ | Minimum separable depth | m |
| 11 | $\mathrm{Lateral\ Resolution} \approx \lambda \cdot \frac{f\#}{D}$ | Beam width limited resolution | m |
| 12 | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Depth calculation from echo time | m |

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> The interplay of these properties defines the **fundamental clinical trade-off in ultrasound**:
> - **Higher frequency → better resolution, but less penetration.**
> - **Lower frequency → deeper penetration, but coarser resolution.**
>
> This is why a radiologist chooses a **2–5 MHz curvilinear probe** for abdominal imaging (deep, large organs) and a **10–15 MHz linear probe** for vascular/thyroid imaging (superficial, fine detail). Every clinical choice is a deliberate balancing of the properties derived above.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Acoustic Impedance and Reflection

We will derive the **intensity reflection coefficient** for a planar ultrasound wave striking a perpendicular interface between two tissues.

**Step 1 — Define the acoustic pressure at an interface.**
When an incident pressure wave $p_i$ strikes an interface, it produces a reflected pressure $p_r$ and a transmitted pressure $p_t$. Conservation of acoustic pressure at the interface gives:

$$
p_i + p_r = p_t
$$

**Step 2 — Apply continuity of particle velocity.**
The product $\rho c$ (impedance) governs how particle velocity relates to pressure. Continuity of particle velocity $u$ across the interface demands:

$$
\frac{p_i}{Z_1} - \frac{p_r}{Z_1} = \frac{p_t}{Z_2}
$$

> This comes from the fact that forward-traveling waves have $u = p/Z$, and reflected waves have $u = -p/Z$ (opposite direction).

**Step 3 — Solve for the pressure reflection coefficient.**
Let $r_p = p_r / p_i$. From Step 1: $p_t = p_i(1 + r_p)$.
Substitute into Step 2:

$$
\frac{p_i}{Z_1}(1 - r_p) = \frac{p_i(1 + r_p)}{Z_2}
$$

Cancel $p_i$ on both sides and cross-multiply:

$$
Z_2(1 - r_p) = Z_1(1 + r_p)
$$

Expand:

$$
Z_2 - Z_2 r_p = Z_1 + Z_1 r_p
$$

Group $r_p$ terms on one side:

$$
Z_2 - Z_1 = r_p(Z_1 + Z_2)
$$

Therefore:

$$
r_p = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to intensity reflection coefficient.**
Intensity is proportional to the square of the pressure amplitude:

$$
R = r_p^2 = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2
$$

> This is the **final, KTU-board-standard formula** for the intensity reflection coefficient at a perpendicular interface.

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (KTU exam style).**
Consider ultrasound travelling from **fat** ($Z_1 = 1.38 \times 10^6$ Rayl) into **muscle** ($Z_2 = 1.70 \times 10^6$ Rayl).

$$
R = \left(\frac{1.70 - 1.38}{1.70 + 1.38}\right)^2 \times 10^{12} / 10^{12} = \left(\frac{0.32}{3.08}\right)^2
$$

$$
R = (0.1039)^2 = 0.0108
$$

Therefore, **~1.08% of the intensity is reflected**; the remaining **~98.92% is transmitted** into the muscle.

### 3.2 Derivation: Depth Calculation from Echo Time

**Step 1 — Relate wavelength, frequency, and speed.**
The fundamental wave equation:

$$
c = f \cdot \lambda
$$

**Step 2 — Express depth in terms of round-trip time.**
The pulse travels **to the reflector and back** to the transducer. Total path length = $2d$, where $d$ is the reflector depth. Time taken:

$$
t = \frac{2d}{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for depth.**

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**
A reflector returns an echo at $t = 26\ \mu s$ in soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s).

$$
d = \frac{1540 \times 26 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.04004}{2} = 0.02002\ \mathrm{m} \approx 2.0\ \mathrm{cm}
$$

The reflector lies at a depth of **~2 cm**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Axial Resolution Limit

**Step 1 — Define axial resolution.**
Axial resolution is the **minimum separation along the beam axis** at which two reflectors can be distinguished as separate.

**Step 2 — Recognize the constraint.**
Two reflectors appear separate **only if the second echo returns after the first pulse has ended**. A pulse contains at least **two cycles** (practical minimum), so its spatial length is $2\lambda$.

**Step 3 — Set up the geometric constraint.**
For the trailing edge of pulse 1 to return before the leading edge of pulse 2 is emitted, the round-trip distance to the deeper reflector must exceed $2\lambda$. Solving for minimum resolvable depth separation:

$$
\Delta d_{min} = \frac{2\lambda}{2} = \lambda
$$

But recognizing that for one pulse to clear the other:

$$
\Delta d_{min} = \frac{c}{2 f}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation.**
At $f = 5$ MHz in soft tissue:

$$
\Delta d_{min} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6} = 1.54 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{m} = 0.154\ \mathrm{mm}
$$

At $f = 10$ MHz:

$$
\Delta d_{min} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 10 \times 10^6} = 0.077\ \mathrm{mm}
$$

This quantitatively proves that **higher frequency = better axial resolution**.

### 3.4 Attenuation: Round-Trip Intensity Drop

**Step 1 — Single-pass attenuation.**
The intensity falls exponentially with distance:

$$
I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x}
$$

where **α** is the linear attenuation coefficient (Np/m). In ultrasound, α is often expressed in **dB/cm**, and the relationship to the dB coefficient $\alpha_{dB}$ is:

$$
\alpha_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(e) \cdot \alpha \approx 8.686\ \alpha
$$

**Step 2 — Round-trip consideration.**
The echo must travel **into tissue and back**, so the total path is $2x$:

$$
I_{echo} = I_0 \cdot e^{-2 \alpha x}
$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to dB scale (more useful clinically).**

$$
\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 2 \cdot \alpha_{dB/cm/MHz} \cdot x\ (\mathrm{cm}) \cdot f\ (\mathrm{MHz})
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**
A 5 MHz pulse penetrates 8 cm into soft tissue and returns. Using the rule of thumb $\alpha = 1$ dB/cm/MHz:

$$
\text{Total Attenuation} = 2 \times 1 \times 8 \times 5 = 80\ \mathrm{dB}
$$

This means only $10^{-8}$ of the original intensity returns — which is why scanners must use **Time Gain Compensation (TGC)** and sensitive receivers.

### 3.5 Penetration Depth vs Frequency Trade-Off

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum imaging depth** is reached when round-trip attenuation equals the maximum dynamic range of the scanner (~100 dB total system budget). Solving:
>
> $$
> d_{max} = \frac{100\ \mathrm{dB}}{2 \cdot \alpha \cdot f}
> $$
>
> For soft tissue ($\alpha = 1$ dB/cm/MHz):
> - $f = 2$ MHz → $d_{max} = 25$ cm (abdominal)
> - $f = 5$ MHz → $d_{max} = 10$ cm (general)
> - $f = 10$ MHz → $d_{max} = 5$ cm (vascular)
> - $f = 15$ MHz → $d_{max} \approx 3.3$ cm (superficial)

---

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Propagation Mechanism of Ultrasound (Longitudinal Pressure Wave)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Transducer Face] -->|Push - Compression| B[High Pressure Region]
    B -->|Particle Oscillation| C[Rest Position]
    C -->|Pull - Rarefaction| D[Low Pressure Region]
    D -->|Particle Oscillation| E[Rest Position]
    E -->|Next Compression Cycle| F[High Pressure Region]
    F -->|Wave Energy Propagates Forward| G[Deep Tissue Reflector]

    H[Mechanical Vibration Source] --> A
    G -->|Echo Returns| I[Transducer Receiver]

    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#E94B3C,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#7B61FF,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Interaction of Ultrasound with Tissue Interfaces

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Pulser and Transmitter] --> TX[Transducer Piezoelectric Crystal]
    TX -->|Ultrasound Beam Emitted| M1[Medium 1 - Fat]
    M1 -->|Partial Reflection| RX1[Echo 1 - Small Z mismatch]
    M1 -->|Partial Transmission| M2[Medium 2 - Liver]
    M2 -->|Partial Reflection| RX2[Echo 2 - Larger Z mismatch]
    M2 -->|Partial Transmission| M3[Medium 3 - Bone]
    M3 -->|Strong Reflection| RX3[Echo 3 - Hyperechoic White Line]
    M3 -->|Negligible Transmission| ABS[Attenuation and Absorption]
    RX1 --> AMP[Receiver Amplifier with TGC]
    RX2 --> AMP
    RX3 --> AMP
    AMP --> DSP[Signal Processing and Scan Conversion]
    DSP --> MON[Diagnostic Image Display]
    ABS --> HEAT[Heat Generation in Tissue]

    style P fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style TX fill:#7B61FF,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style M1 fill:#F8E71C,stroke:#000
    style M2 fill:#F8E71C,stroke:#000
    style M3 fill:#F8E71C,stroke:#000
    style RX1 fill:#E94B3C,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style RX2 fill:#E94B3C,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style RX3 fill:#E94B3C,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style AMP fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style DSP fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style MON fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style HEAT fill:#B8E986,stroke:#000
```

### 4.3 Frequency vs Penetration vs Resolution Trade-Off Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{What is the clinical target?}
    Q1 -->|Deep Abdominal Organs| LF[Low Frequency 2 to 5 MHz]
    Q1 -->|General and Obstetric| MF[Medium Frequency 5 to 10 MHz]
    Q1 -->|Superficial Vascular and Thyroid| HF[High Frequency 10 to 15 MHz]
    Q1 -->|Intravascular Micro Imaging| VHF[Very High Frequency 20 to 50 MHz]

    LF --> LP[Deep Penetration up to 25 cm]
    LF --> LR[Lower Axial Resolution about 0.3 to 0.75 mm]
    MF --> MP[Medium Penetration 10 to 15 cm]
    MF --> MR[Medium Resolution 0.15 to 0.30 mm]
    HF --> SP[Shallow Penetration 3 to 6 cm]
    HF --> HR[High Resolution 0.08 to 0.15 mm]
    VHF --> XP[Very Shallow Penetration less than 1 cm]
    VHF --> XR[Ultra High Resolution less than 0.04 mm]

    style Q1 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000
    style LF fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style MF fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000,color:#fff

```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q1. Define acoustic impedance. State its SI unit and write the formula.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Acoustic impedance (Z) is the product of the density of a medium and the velocity of ultrasound in that medium. It represents the resistance offered by the medium to the propagation of the ultrasound wave. **Formula:** $Z = \rho \cdot c$, where ρ is density (kg/m³) and c is the speed of sound (m/s). **SI unit:** Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹). For soft tissue, the standard reference value is **1.63 × 10⁶ Rayl**.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> **Q2. List the five main properties of ultrasound and state which property is responsible for image formation in B-mode imaging.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The five main properties of ultrasound are: **(i) Propagation speed**, **(ii) Reflection**, **(iii) Refraction**, **(iv) Scattering**, and **(v) Attenuation**. **Reflection** is the property primarily responsible for image formation in B-mode (Brightness mode) imaging — echoes returning from tissue interfaces are mapped to grayscale pixel brightness based on echo amplitude and round-trip time.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to use the round-trip factor of 2** in depth and attenuation calculations. Always verify whether the problem is one-way (transmission) or round-trip (echo).
> - Many candidates write **only the formula for pressure reflection** ($r_p$) instead of the **intensity reflection** ($R = r_p^2$). The KTU board strictly expects $R$ in dB/fraction form, not $r_p$.
> - Do **not** confuse the **attenuation coefficient in dB/cm** with the **linear absorption coefficient in Np/m**. Conversion: $\alpha_{dB} = 8.686 \cdot \alpha_{Np}$.
> - When stating values like "speed of sound in soft tissue," always mention **1540 m/s as the standardized reference** — not the 1500 m/s often used in physics problems. KTU medical imaging questions demand the clinical value.

---

#### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — Acoustic Impedance and Reflection

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OEEBT723, Module 1]**
>
> **(a)** Define characteristic acoustic impedance. Derive the intensity reflection coefficient at a tissue interface. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An ultrasound beam travels from liver ($Z_1 = 1.66 \times 10^6$ Rayl) into kidney ($Z_2 = 1.62 \times 10^6$ Rayl). Calculate the intensity reflection and transmission coefficients. Comment on the clinical significance of such an interface. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
*(Full derivation as shown in Section 3.1 above.)*
1. Definition: $Z = \rho \cdot c$, unit Rayl — **1 mark**
2. Continuity of pressure at interface: $p_i + p_r = p_t$ — **1 mark**
3. Continuity of particle velocity — **1 mark**
4. Derivation of $r_p = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$ — **3 marks**
5. Final formula $R = r_p^2$ — **1 mark**

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Step 1 — Substitute the values:

$$
R = \left(\frac{1.62 \times 10^6 - 1.66 \times 10^6}{1.62 \times 10^6 + 1.66 \times 10^6}\right)^2
$$

Step 2 — Compute the numerator and denominator:

$$
R = \left(\frac{-0.04 \times 10^6}{3.28 \times 10^6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{-0.04}{3.28}\right)^2
$$

Step 3 — Evaluate:

$$
R = (-0.01220)^2 = 1.49 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0149\%
$$

Step 4 — Transmission coefficient:

$$
T = 1 - R = 1 - 0.000149 \approx 0.99985 = 99.985\%
$$

Step 5 — Clinical comment (2 marks):
The liver–kidney interface produces a **very small echo (only ~0.015% of intensity reflected)** because the acoustic impedances of the two organs are nearly identical. Clinically, this means the boundary between liver and kidney is **poorly visualized** on standard B-mode ultrasound, and a small amount of "through-transmission" enhancement may be observed posterior to the kidney (since minimal energy is lost at the interface).

---

#### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Attenuation, Depth, and Resolution

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OEEBT723, Module 1]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of attenuation of ultrasound in tissue. State the rule-of-thumb attenuation coefficient for soft tissue and show that a 5 MHz beam has a maximum practical imaging depth of approximately 10 cm. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of relevant formulas, derive the axial resolution of an ultrasound system. If a 7.5 MHz linear probe is used, calculate the axial resolution in soft tissue. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
1. Definition: Attenuation is the progressive loss of intensity as the beam propagates, due to absorption, scattering, and beam divergence — **2 marks**
2. Rule of thumb: $\alpha \approx 1$ dB/cm/MHz for soft tissue — **1 mark**
3. Round-trip attenuation equation: $\text{Attenuation}_{dB} = 2 \cdot \alpha \cdot x \cdot f$ — **2 marks**
4. For 5 MHz, max depth ~10 cm:
   - Assume total system dynamic range = 100 dB
   - $100 = 2 \times 1 \times d \times 5$ → $d = 10$ cm — **2 marks**

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
1. Definition of axial resolution — **1 mark**
2. Pulse length consideration: minimum 2 cycles — **1 mark**
3. Derivation: $\Delta d_{min} = c/(2f)$ — **3 marks**
4. Calculation for 7.5 MHz:

$$
\Delta d_{min} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 7.5 \times 10^6} = 1.027 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{m} \approx 0.103\ \mathrm{mm}
$$

— **2 marks**

This is the **theoretical maximum resolution**; practical systems achieve about 2–3× this value due to bandwidth limitations and focusing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Properties of Ultrasound (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Ultrasound is a longitudinal mechanical pressure wave** requiring a medium; frequency > 20 kHz.
- [ ] **Diagnostic frequency range:** 2 MHz – 15 MHz (general); up to 50 MHz for intravascular imaging.
- [ ] **Standard speed of sound in soft tissue:** **c = 1540 m/s** (memorize this value).
- [ ] **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho \cdot c$, unit **Rayl**. Soft tissue ≈ 1.63 × 10⁶ Rayl.
- [ ] **Intensity reflection coefficient:** $R = \left[(Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)\right]^2$ — note the **square**.
- [ ] **Depth formula:** $d = c \cdot t / 2$ — always use the **round-trip factor of 2**.
- [ ] **Attenuation rule:** $\alpha \approx 1$ dB/cm/MHz in soft tissue. Round-trip: $2\alpha xf$.
- [ ] **Trade-off:** Higher frequency → better resolution, lower penetration. Lower frequency → deeper penetration, coarser resolution.
- [ ] **Bone and air** are near-perfect reflectors → cause acoustic shadowing.
- [ ] **Red blood cells (RBCs)** cause Rayleigh scattering — basis of Doppler ultrasound.
- [ ] **Scattering intensity** $\propto f^4$ — high-frequency probes yield finer speckle texture.
- [ ] **Axial resolution** $\Delta d = c/(2f)$ — improves linearly with frequency.
- [ ] **Refraction** follows Snell's law: $\sin\theta_1 / c_1 = \sin\theta_2 / c_2$.
- [ ] The clinical choice of probe frequency is **always** a deliberate resolution-vs-penetration compromise.
- [ ] Remember: $c = f \cdot \lambda$ is the **master equation** for all depth and resolution problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Doppler Effect and Principle of Image Formation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Doppler Effect and Principle of Image Formation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Doppler Effect (Formal Definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Doppler Effect (KTU 2024 Definition):** The Doppler Effect in medical imaging is the apparent shift in the observed frequency of an acoustic wave when there is relative motion between the ultrasound source (transducer) and the reflecting/scattering medium (typically red blood cells). This frequency shift is directly proportional to the velocity of the moving reflector and forms the fundamental physical basis of all Doppler-based diagnostic ultrasound systems.

The phenomenon was first described by **Christian Johann Doppler** in **1842** for electromagnetic waves, and subsequently verified acoustically by **C.H.D. Buys Ballot** in **1845** using a moving train of trumpeters. In clinical ultrasound, the relevant moving reflectors are **erythrocytes (RBCs)** flowing within blood vessels.

**Standard Physical Constants Used (must be memorized):**

- Velocity of ultrasound in soft tissue: **c ≈ 1540 m/s**
- Density of blood: **ρ_blood ≈ 1060 kg/m³**
- Typical diagnostic frequency range: **2 MHz – 15 MHz**
- Typical Doppler shift range (clinical): **20 Hz – 20 kHz** (audible band)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Doppler shift formula and its dependency on the **insonation angle (θ)** is a *favourite* KTU question topic. A common pitfall is forgetting that the Doppler system works as a **transmitter AND receiver** (round-trip effect), introducing a factor of **2** in the equation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Ambulance Analogy

Imagine you are standing on a footpath and an ambulance with a blaring siren approaches you. As it comes closer, the siren sounds **higher pitched** (frequency increases), and as it speeds away, the siren suddenly drops to a **lower pitch** (frequency decreases).

In medical Doppler ultrasound, the ambulance is the **transducer** and the **blood cells** act like a fleet of tiny ambulances. The "siren" is the high-frequency sound wave (typically 5–10 MHz for vascular studies). The shift in pitch that your ear detects is analogous to the **electrical frequency shift** that the ultrasound machine precisely measures. A positive shift means blood is flowing **toward** the probe; a negative shift means it is flowing **away**.

## 1.3 Principle of Image Formation (Formal Definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle of Ultrasound Image Formation:** Medical ultrasound images are formed by transmitting short-duration acoustic pulses into tissue, recording the **time-of-flight** and **amplitude** of the echoes that return from acoustic-impedance discontinuities, and then mapping these into a 1-D, 2-D, or 3-D spatial representation of internal anatomical structures.

The process is governed by three pillars:
1. **Pulse-Echo Principle** – transmit, wait, receive.
2. **Acoustic Impedance Mismatch** – echoes only occur where tissue properties change.
3. **Time-of-Flight Ranging** – depth = (c × t) / 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acoustic Impedance (Z)** is the product of tissue density and the speed of sound in that tissue: **Z = ρ × c**, with units of **Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹)**. Without an impedance difference, no echo is generated, no matter how powerful the pulse.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Doppler Ultrasound Systems

KTU expects students to distinguish between these three primary modes:

| Display Mode | Full Name | What It Displays | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A-Mode** | Amplitude Mode | 1-D spikes; height ∝ echo strength | Ophthalmology, basic ranging |
| **B-Mode** | Brightness Mode | 2-D grayscale cross-section | General anatomical imaging |
| **M-Mode** | Motion Mode | B-mode line vs. time | Cardiac valve motion |
| **CW Doppler** | Continuous Wave | Velocity spectrum (no depth gating) | High-velocity jets |
| **PW Doppler** | Pulsed Wave | Velocity at a specific sample volume | Vascular flow profiling |
| **Color Doppler** | — | 2-D color overlay of velocity | Vascular mapping |
| **Spectral Doppler** | — | Velocity vs. time waveform | Quantitative flow analysis |

## 2.2 Why a Factor of 2 Appears in Medical Doppler

The classical Doppler effect (e.g., for a moving train whistle) involves a **single** moving source and a **stationary observer**:

$$f_{observed} = f_0 \left( \frac{c}{c - v_s} \right)$$

However, in medical ultrasound, the situation is **doubly dynamic**:
1. The **transducer** transmits a wave of frequency $f_0$ to a moving RBC.
2. The RBC acts as a **moving receiver**, perceiving a shifted frequency.
3. The RBC **re-radiates** (scatters) this new frequency.
4. The **transducer** (now acting as a moving receiver) perceives a *second* shift.

When $v \ll c$, the first-order (non-relativistic) approximation of the round-trip effect yields:

$$f_d = f_{received} - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}$$

This **factor of 2** is the single most important mathematical signature of medical Doppler.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Note:** Vertical bars (for absolute value) are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. All formulas below are mandatory for KTU board examinations.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $c$ = speed of sound (m/s), $Z$ in Rayl |
| 2 | Reflection Coefficient (Intensity) | $R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$ | Dimensionless; 0 to 1 |
| 3 | Transmission Coefficient (Intensity) | $T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | Dimensionless; 0 to 1 |
| 4 | Note: $R + T = 1$ | — | Conservation of energy |
| 5 | Depth of Reflector | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $t$ = round-trip time (s), factor 2 for round trip |
| 6 | Doppler Shift (Medical) | $f_d = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}$ | $f_0$ (Hz), $v$ (m/s), $\theta$ (rad), $c$ (m/s) |
| 7 | Velocity from Doppler | $v = \frac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | Rearrangement of (6) |
| 8 | Aliasing Limit (Nyquist) | $f_d \le \frac{PRF}{2}$ | $PRF$ = Pulse Repetition Frequency |
| 9 | Axial Resolution | $\text{Res}_{axial} = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | $\tau$ = pulse duration (s) |
| 10 | Attenuation (soft tissue) | $\alpha \approx 0.5 \text{ dB/cm/MHz}$ | Approx. average; frequency-dependent |
| 11 | Cosine of Insonation Angle | $\cos\theta$ | $\theta$ = angle between beam & flow |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hemodynamic Diagnosis:** Detecting stenosis, regurgitation, aneurysms.
- **Obstetrics:** Umbilical-cord and fetal-middle-cerebral-artery flow assessment.
- **Cardiology:** Ejection fraction, valve-area planimetry.
- **Industry analogue:** The same physics powers **radar guns** (RF), **LIDAR speed guns** (optical), and **sonar** on submarines.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Medical Doppler Equation

We consider the general case: a stationary transducer transmits a wave of frequency $f_0$ to a reflector (RBC) moving with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ to the beam axis.

**Step 1 — General Doppler equation for a moving observer:**

The general (non-relativistic) Doppler effect when a source of frequency $f_0$ is stationary and an observer moves with velocity component $v_o$ *toward* the source is:

$$f_{obs} = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v_o}{c} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Project the RBC velocity onto the beam direction:**

The RBC approaches or recedes from the transducer along the beam axis. The relevant velocity component is $v \cos\theta$ (projection of $\vec{v}$ onto the beam axis).

For a stationary transducer, the RBC perceives:

$$f_1 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right)$$

**Step 3 — The RBC re-radiates this frequency:**

The RBC now acts as a *source* moving with velocity component $v \cos\theta$ *away* from (or toward) the stationary transducer. The frequency re-radiated is $f_1$, and the transducer (observer) receives:

$$f_2 = f_1 \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

Substituting $f_1$:

$$f_2 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right) \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Combine the fractions:**

$$f_2 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 5 — The Doppler shift is the difference:**

$$f_d = f_2 - f_0 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} - 1 \right)$$

$$f_d = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta - (c - v \cos\theta)}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

$$f_d = f_0 \left( \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the medical approximation $v \ll c$:**

Since blood flow velocity ($v \le 5$ m/s) is far smaller than the speed of sound in tissue ($c \approx 1540$ m/s), the term $v \cos\theta$ in the denominator is negligible. The denominator reduces to $c$:

$$f_d \approx f_0 \cdot \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

$$\boxed{f_d = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Depth from Time of Flight

**Step 1:** The pulse travels down to the reflector in time $t_1$ at speed $c$:

$$t_1 = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 2:** The echo travels back up to the transducer in time $t_2$ at the same speed:

$$t_2 = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 3:** The *round-trip* time measured by the system is:

$$t = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{2d}{c}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for depth $d$:

$$\boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** If a reflector is at $d = 1$ cm = 0.01 m deep, the round-trip time is $t = 2 \times 0.01 / 1540 \approx 13$ microseconds. This is why ultrasound machines need microsecond-precision timing.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 5 MHz transducer is used to image a vessel. The insonation angle is 60°. If the measured Doppler shift is 1.5 kHz, calculate the blood-flow velocity. ($c = 1540$ m/s).

**Solution:**

Given: $f_0 = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz, $f_d = 1.5 \times 10^3$ Hz, $\theta = 60°$, $c = 1540$ m/s.

From the velocity formula:

$$v = \frac{f_d \cdot c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$$

Substituting:

$$v = \frac{1.5 \times 10^3 \times 1540}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times \cos 60°}$$

$$v = \frac{2.31 \times 10^6}{10^{7} \times 0.5} = \frac{2.31 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{v = 0.462 \text{ m/s} \approx 46.2 \text{ cm/s}}$$

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Doppler Ultrasound System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Master Clock and Pulser] --> B[Transducer Probe]
    B --> C[Beam Forming and Steering Network]
    C --> D[Patient Tissue and Blood Volume]
    D --> E[Returning Echoes and Doppler Shifted Signal]
    E --> F[Receive Beamformer]
    F --> G[RF Signal Demodulation Stage]
    G --> H[Wall Filter High Pass Clutter Removal]
    H --> I[Spectral Analysis FFT Processor]
    I --> J[Display Processor and Color Map Encoder]
    J --> K[Clinical Monitor Output]
    G --> L[Envelope Detector for B Mode]
    L --> M[Scan Converter Memory]
    M --> K
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Image Formation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pulser Excitation 100 to 200 Volt Step] --> B[PZT Crystal Transmit Burst]
    B --> C[Tissue Propagation c equals 1540 m per second]
    C --> D[Acoustic Impedance Boundaries Generate Echoes]
    D --> E[Round Trip Time of Flight Measurement]
    E --> F[Depth Calculation d equals c t divided by 2]
    F --> G[Signal Processing Amplification TGC Compression]
    G --> H[Scan Conversion to Cartesian Pixels]
    H --> I[Display on Monitor as B Mode Image]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Doppler Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Doppler Examination] --> B{Continuous or Pulsed}
    B -->|Continuous| C[Use Two Crystals TX and RX]
    B -->|Pulsed| D[Use Single Crystal Alternating]
    C --> E[No Depth Discrimination]
    D --> F[Range Gate Selects Sample Volume]
    F --> G{Shift Exceeds Nyquist}
    G -->|Yes| H[Increase PRF or Shift Baseline]
    G -->|No| I[Display Spectrum]
    H --> I
    E --> J[Audio Output via Speaker]
    I --> J
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define the Doppler Effect. Why is the angle of insonation (θ) important in clinical Doppler ultrasound?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Doppler Effect is the change in observed frequency of a wave due to relative motion between the source and observer. In medical ultrasound, it is the frequency shift between transmitted and reflected waves caused by moving red blood cells.

The clinical Doppler equation is:

$$f_d = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}$$

The angle $\theta$ is critical because:
1. When $\theta = 0°$, $\cos\theta = 1$ and the system gives the **true maximum velocity**.
2. When $\theta = 90°$, $\cos\theta = 0$ and **no Doppler shift is detected** even if flow exists. This is the **"Doppler angle blind spot."**
3. Clinically, the operator must keep $\theta \le 60°$ to avoid large errors; values of 60–90° cause unstable, unreliable readings.
4. Therefore, the angle must be **measured and corrected** by aligning the beam parallel to the vessel as closely as possible.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks explanation of θ importance.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Repeated Pattern]`

**What is acoustic impedance? Mention its significance in ultrasound image formation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Acoustic impedance $Z$ is the product of the density ($\rho$) of a medium and the velocity ($c$) of sound in that medium:

$$Z = \rho \cdot c$$

It is measured in **Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹)**.

**Significance in image formation:**
1. **Echo generation:** A pulse-echo image is formed only when an acoustic wave encounters a boundary between two tissues of *different* acoustic impedance.
2. **Reflection coefficient** at such a boundary is:

$$R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$

3. **Large mismatch ⇒ strong echo:** For example, the soft-tissue / air interface has $Z_{tissue} \approx 1.6 \times 10^6$ Rayl vs. $Z_{air} \approx 0.0004 \times 10^6$ Rayl, producing a near-100% reflection (used to explain why gel is required!).
4. **Small mismatch ⇒ transmission:** Most soft tissues have $Z$ values close to each other, so most energy is transmitted, allowing deep imaging.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark image-formation significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Derive the medical Doppler shift equation $f_d = \dfrac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}$, clearly stating the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3.5 MHz transducer is used to measure blood flow. The angle between the beam and vessel is 45°. The detected Doppler shift is 800 Hz. Calculate: (i) the blood-flow velocity, and (ii) the fractional error introduced if the operator mistakenly assumes the angle is 30°. ($c = 1540$ m/s) **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Setup and identification of moving observer (2 marks):**
The transducer (stationary) emits $f_0$. The RBC acts as a *moving observer* approaching with velocity component $v \cos\theta$:

$$f_1 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Re-emission and moving source effect (2 marks):**
The RBC re-radiates at $f_1$ while moving *away* from the transducer, so the transducer receives:

$$f_2 = f_1 \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right) = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Subtraction and simplification (2 marks):**
The Doppler shift is the difference $f_d = f_2 - f_0$:

$$f_d = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta - c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \right) = f_0 \left( \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Approximation and final form (1 mark):**
Since $v \ll c$ for blood flow, the denominator simplifies to $c$:

$$\boxed{f_d = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}}$$

**Assumptions (must be stated):**
- $v \ll c$ (non-relativistic)
- $\theta$ is the angle between beam and flow direction
- Reflector is a point scatterer (valid for RBC ensembles)
- Homogeneous medium of sound speed $c$

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

**Part (i) — True velocity (3 marks):**

$$v = \frac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0 \cos 45°} = \frac{800 \times 1540}{2 \times 3.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.7071}$$

$$v = \frac{1.232 \times 10^6}{4.95 \times 10^6} \approx 0.249 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{true} \approx 24.9 \text{ cm/s}}$$

**Part (ii) — Apparent velocity at wrong angle (4 marks):**

If operator assumes $\theta = 30°$:

$$v_{wrong} = \frac{800 \times 1540}{2 \times 3.5 \times 10^6 \times \cos 30°} = \frac{1.232 \times 10^6}{6.062 \times 10^6} \approx 0.203 \text{ m/s}$$

**Fractional error:**

$$\text{Error} = \frac{v_{true} - v_{wrong}}{v_{true}} = \frac{0.249 - 0.203}{0.249} \approx 0.184$$

$$\boxed{\text{Error} \approx 18.4\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. Forgetting to write the **assumptions** in the derivation part: **–1 mark**.
> 2. Mixing up the position of $\cos\theta$ (writing it in the numerator when rearranging): **–1 mark**.
> 3. In numerical problems, writing the unit in **cm/s** but dividing in **m/s** without conversion: **–0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 4. Not justifying why the denominator $c - v\cos\theta \approx c$ — examiners *always* expect this justification: **–0.5 mark**.
> 5. Using $c = 1500$ or $c = 340$ (speed in air) instead of **1540 m/s** for soft tissue: **–0.5 mark** (unless the problem specifies otherwise).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **pulse-echo principle** in detail and derive the relation between depth and time of flight. Discuss how A-mode, B-mode, and M-mode displays are obtained from the same principle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define acoustic impedance. Two media have acoustic impedances $Z_1 = 1.63 \times 10^6$ Rayl and $Z_2 = 1.70 \times 10^6$ Rayl respectively. Find: (i) the reflection coefficient, (ii) the transmission coefficient, and (iii) the percentage of incident intensity reflected. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

**Pulse-Echo Principle (3 marks):**
The transducer is excited by a short high-voltage pulse (typically 100–200 V, duration 0.5–2 µs). This pulse drives the piezoelectric crystal, converting electrical energy into an acoustic wave that propagates into the tissue. The crystal is then switched to a *receive* mode. Echoes returning from tissue interfaces excite the crystal, producing small voltages that are amplified, processed, and displayed.

**Depth Derivation (2 marks):**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

(As derived in Section 3.2 of these notes.)

**Display Modes (2 marks):**
- **A-mode:** The amplified echo signal is displayed as vertical spikes on a horizontal time-base. Spike height = echo amplitude; spike position = depth.
- **B-mode:** The amplitude signal modulates the **brightness** of a spot on the display, while the beam is scanned across the tissue to build a 2-D grayscale image.
- **M-mode:** A single B-mode line is displayed repeatedly as time advances, producing a "strip chart" of moving structures (e.g., heart valves).

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $Z_1 = 1.63 \times 10^6$ Rayl, $Z_2 = 1.70 \times 10^6$ Rayl.

**Part (i) — Reflection coefficient (2.5 marks):**

$$R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{1.70 - 1.63}{1.70 + 1.63} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{0.07}{3.33} \right)^2$$

$$R = (0.02102)^2 \approx 4.42 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.000442 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

**Part (ii) — Transmission coefficient (2.5 marks):**

$$T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = \frac{4 \times 1.63 \times 1.70}{(1.63 + 1.70)^2} = \frac{11.084}{11.0889} \approx 0.9996$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 0.99956}$$

**Part (iii) — Percentage of reflected intensity (2 marks):**

$$\text{Reflected \%} = R \times 100\% \approx 0.0442\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Approximately 0.044\% of the incident intensity is reflected.}}$$

*This very small reflection explains why ultrasound can image deep structures — most of the energy is transmitted forward.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Numerical Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Writing $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ (the **amplitude** reflection coefficient) instead of the **intensity** reflection coefficient with the square: this is a **1-mark penalty**.
> 2. Forgetting that $R + T = 1$ (a built-in self-check; if yours does not add to 1, you have an arithmetic error).
> 3. Mixing up $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ — since the formula uses $Z_2 - Z_1$, the result is the same after squaring, but sloppy work loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Summary — Master These Before Walking Into the KTU Exam:**

- [x] **Doppler Equation (memorize verbatim):** $f_d = \dfrac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}$
- [x] **Speed of sound in soft tissue:** $c = 1540$ m/s (NOT 340 m/s)
- [x] **Factor of 2 reason:** Round-trip motion — RBC is both observer and re-emitter.
- [x] **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$, in **Rayl**.
- [x] **Intensity reflection coefficient:** $R = \left( \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$
- [x] **Intensity transmission coefficient:** $T = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$
- [x] **Conservation law:** $R + T = 1$ (always!)
- [x] **Depth formula:** $d = \dfrac{c t}{2}$ (the 2 is for round trip).
- [x] **CW vs. PW Doppler:** CW has *no* depth discrimination; PW has *one* sample volume.
- [x] **Aliasing:** Occurs when $f_d > PRF/2$ (Nyquist limit).
- [x] **Display modes:** A = Amplitude spike, B = Brightness 2-D, M = Motion time-trace.
- [x] **Optimal Doppler angle:** $\theta \le 60°$ clinically; **90° = blind spot**.
- [x] **Attenuation rule of thumb:** $\alpha \approx 0.5$ dB/cm/MHz in soft tissue.
- [x] **Discovered by:** Christian Johann Doppler, **1842**.
- [x] **Common Examiner Trap:** Students forget the $\cos\theta$ in numericals and end up with velocities 2–3× too high.

**Mnemonic for modes — "A-B-M" = "Amplitude – Brightness – Motion"** — write this down first on your answer sheet to anchor your memory for the rest of the question.

**Golden Rule for KTU Derivations:** *Always state assumptions, always show the approximation $v \ll c$, and never skip a step. Examiners award method marks generously only if intermediate steps are visible.*

---

*End of Notes — Doppler Effect and Principle of Image Formation (Module 1, OEEBT723).*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Doppler Ultrasound Systems

KTU expects students to distinguish between these three primary modes:

| Display Mode | Full Name | What It Displays | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A-Mode** | Amplitude Mode | 1-D spikes; height ∝ echo strength | Ophthalmology, basic ranging |
| **B-Mode** | Brightness Mode | 2-D grayscale cross-section | General anatomical imaging |
| **M-Mode** | Motion Mode | B-mode line vs. time | Cardiac valve motion |
| **CW Doppler** | Continuous Wave | Velocity spectrum (no depth gating) | High-velocity jets |
| **PW Doppler** | Pulsed Wave | Velocity at a specific sample volume | Vascular flow profiling |
| **Color Doppler** | — | 2-D color overlay of velocity | Vascular mapping |
| **Spectral Doppler** | — | Velocity vs. time waveform | Quantitative flow analysis |

## 2.2 Why a Factor of 2 Appears in Medical Doppler

The classical Doppler effect (e.g., for a moving train whistle) involves a **single** moving source and a **stationary observer**:

$$f_{observed} = f_0 \left( \frac{c}{c - v_s} \right)$$

However, in medical ultrasound, the situation is **doubly dynamic**:
1. The **transducer** transmits a wave of frequency $f_0$ to a moving RBC.
2. The RBC acts as a **moving receiver**, perceiving a shifted frequency.
3. The RBC **re-radiates** (scatters) this new frequency.
4. The **transducer** (now acting as a moving receiver) perceives a *second* shift.

When $v \ll c$, the first-order (non-relativistic) approximation of the round-trip effect yields:

$$f_d = f_{received} - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}$$

This **factor of 2** is the single most important mathematical signature of medical Doppler.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Note:** Vertical bars (for absolute value) are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. All formulas below are mandatory for KTU board examinations.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $c$ = speed of sound (m/s), $Z$ in Rayl |
| 2 | Reflection Coefficient (Intensity) | $R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$ | Dimensionless; 0 to 1 |
| 3 | Transmission Coefficient (Intensity) | $T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | Dimensionless; 0 to 1 |
| 4 | Note: $R + T = 1$ | — | Conservation of energy |
| 5 | Depth of Reflector | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $t$ = round-trip time (s), factor 2 for round trip |
| 6 | Doppler Shift (Medical) | $f_d = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}$ | $f_0$ (Hz), $v$ (m/s), $\theta$ (rad), $c$ (m/s) |
| 7 | Velocity from Doppler | $v = \frac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | Rearrangement of (6) |
| 8 | Aliasing Limit (Nyquist) | $f_d \le \frac{PRF}{2}$ | $PRF$ = Pulse Repetition Frequency |
| 9 | Axial Resolution | $\text{Res}_{axial} = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | $\tau$ = pulse duration (s) |
| 10 | Attenuation (soft tissue) | $\alpha \approx 0.5 \text{ dB/cm/MHz}$ | Approx. average; frequency-dependent |
| 11 | Cosine of Insonation Angle | $\cos\theta$ | $\theta$ = angle between beam & flow |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hemodynamic Diagnosis:** Detecting stenosis, regurgitation, aneurysms.
- **Obstetrics:** Umbilical-cord and fetal-middle-cerebral-artery flow assessment.
- **Cardiology:** Ejection fraction, valve-area planimetry.
- **Industry analogue:** The same physics powers **radar guns** (RF), **LIDAR speed guns** (optical), and **sonar** on submarines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Medical Doppler Equation

We consider the general case: a stationary transducer transmits a wave of frequency $f_0$ to a reflector (RBC) moving with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ to the beam axis.

**Step 1 — General Doppler equation for a moving observer:**

The general (non-relativistic) Doppler effect when a source of frequency $f_0$ is stationary and an observer moves with velocity component $v_o$ *toward* the source is:

$$f_{obs} = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v_o}{c} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Project the RBC velocity onto the beam direction:**

The RBC approaches or recedes from the transducer along the beam axis. The relevant velocity component is $v \cos\theta$ (projection of $\vec{v}$ onto the beam axis).

For a stationary transducer, the RBC perceives:

$$f_1 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right)$$

**Step 3 — The RBC re-radiates this frequency:**

The RBC now acts as a *source* moving with velocity component $v \cos\theta$ *away* from (or toward) the stationary transducer. The frequency re-radiated is $f_1$, and the transducer (observer) receives:

$$f_2 = f_1 \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

Substituting $f_1$:

$$f_2 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right) \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Combine the fractions:**

$$f_2 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 5 — The Doppler shift is the difference:**

$$f_d = f_2 - f_0 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} - 1 \right)$$

$$f_d = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta - (c - v \cos\theta)}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

$$f_d = f_0 \left( \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the medical approximation $v \ll c$:**

Since blood flow velocity ($v \le 5$ m/s) is far smaller than the speed of sound in tissue ($c \approx 1540$ m/s), the term $v \cos\theta$ in the denominator is negligible. The denominator reduces to $c$:

$$f_d \approx f_0 \cdot \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

$$\boxed{f_d = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Depth from Time of Flight

**Step 1:** The pulse travels down to the reflector in time $t_1$ at speed $c$:

$$t_1 = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 2:** The echo travels back up to the transducer in time $t_2$ at the same speed:

$$t_2 = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 3:** The *round-trip* time measured by the system is:

$$t = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{2d}{c}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for depth $d$:

$$\boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** If a reflector is at $d = 1$ cm = 0.01 m deep, the round-trip time is $t = 2 \times 0.01 / 1540 \approx 13$ microseconds. This is why ultrasound machines need microsecond-precision timing.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 5 MHz transducer is used to image a vessel. The insonation angle is 60°. If the measured Doppler shift is 1.5 kHz, calculate the blood-flow velocity. ($c = 1540$ m/s).

**Solution:**

Given: $f_0 = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz, $f_d = 1.5 \times 10^3$ Hz, $\theta = 60°$, $c = 1540$ m/s.

From the velocity formula:

$$v = \frac{f_d \cdot c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$$

Substituting:

$$v = \frac{1.5 \times 10^3 \times 1540}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times \cos 60°}$$

$$v = \frac{2.31 \times 10^6}{10^{7} \times 0.5} = \frac{2.31 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{v = 0.462 \text{ m/s} \approx 46.2 \text{ cm/s}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Doppler Ultrasound System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Master Clock and Pulser] --> B[Transducer Probe]
    B --> C[Beam Forming and Steering Network]
    C --> D[Patient Tissue and Blood Volume]
    D --> E[Returning Echoes and Doppler Shifted Signal]
    E --> F[Receive Beamformer]
    F --> G[RF Signal Demodulation Stage]
    G --> H[Wall Filter High Pass Clutter Removal]
    H --> I[Spectral Analysis FFT Processor]
    I --> J[Display Processor and Color Map Encoder]
    J --> K[Clinical Monitor Output]
    G --> L[Envelope Detector for B Mode]
    L --> M[Scan Converter Memory]
    M --> K
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Image Formation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pulser Excitation 100 to 200 Volt Step] --> B[PZT Crystal Transmit Burst]
    B --> C[Tissue Propagation c equals 1540 m per second]
    C --> D[Acoustic Impedance Boundaries Generate Echoes]
    D --> E[Round Trip Time of Flight Measurement]
    E --> F[Depth Calculation d equals c t divided by 2]
    F --> G[Signal Processing Amplification TGC Compression]
    G --> H[Scan Conversion to Cartesian Pixels]
    H --> I[Display on Monitor as B Mode Image]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Doppler Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Doppler Examination] --> B{Continuous or Pulsed}
    B -->|Continuous| C[Use Two Crystals TX and RX]
    B -->|Pulsed| D[Use Single Crystal Alternating]
    C --> E[No Depth Discrimination]
    D --> F[Range Gate Selects Sample Volume]
    F --> G{Shift Exceeds Nyquist}
    G -->|Yes| H[Increase PRF or Shift Baseline]
    G -->|No| I[Display Spectrum]
    H --> I
    E --> J[Audio Output via Speaker]
    I --> J
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define the Doppler Effect. Why is the angle of insonation (θ) important in clinical Doppler ultrasound?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Doppler Effect is the change in observed frequency of a wave due to relative motion between the source and observer. In medical ultrasound, it is the frequency shift between transmitted and reflected waves caused by moving red blood cells.

The clinical Doppler equation is:

$$f_d = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}$$

The angle $\theta$ is critical because:
1. When $\theta = 0°$, $\cos\theta = 1$ and the system gives the **true maximum velocity**.
2. When $\theta = 90°$, $\cos\theta = 0$ and **no Doppler shift is detected** even if flow exists. This is the **"Doppler angle blind spot."**
3. Clinically, the operator must keep $\theta \le 60°$ to avoid large errors; values of 60–90° cause unstable, unreliable readings.
4. Therefore, the angle must be **measured and corrected** by aligning the beam parallel to the vessel as closely as possible.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks explanation of θ importance.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Repeated Pattern]`

**What is acoustic impedance? Mention its significance in ultrasound image formation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Acoustic impedance $Z$ is the product of the density ($\rho$) of a medium and the velocity ($c$) of sound in that medium:

$$Z = \rho \cdot c$$

It is measured in **Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹)**.

**Significance in image formation:**
1. **Echo generation:** A pulse-echo image is formed only when an acoustic wave encounters a boundary between two tissues of *different* acoustic impedance.
2. **Reflection coefficient** at such a boundary is:

$$R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$

3. **Large mismatch ⇒ strong echo:** For example, the soft-tissue / air interface has $Z_{tissue} \approx 1.6 \times 10^6$ Rayl vs. $Z_{air} \approx 0.0004 \times 10^6$ Rayl, producing a near-100% reflection (used to explain why gel is required!).
4. **Small mismatch ⇒ transmission:** Most soft tissues have $Z$ values close to each other, so most energy is transmitted, allowing deep imaging.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark image-formation significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Derive the medical Doppler shift equation $f_d = \dfrac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}$, clearly stating the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3.5 MHz transducer is used to measure blood flow. The angle between the beam and vessel is 45°. The detected Doppler shift is 800 Hz. Calculate: (i) the blood-flow velocity, and (ii) the fractional error introduced if the operator mistakenly assumes the angle is 30°. ($c = 1540$ m/s) **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Setup and identification of moving observer (2 marks):**
The transducer (stationary) emits $f_0$. The RBC acts as a *moving observer* approaching with velocity component $v \cos\theta$:

$$f_1 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Re-emission and moving source effect (2 marks):**
The RBC re-radiates at $f_1$ while moving *away* from the transducer, so the transducer receives:

$$f_2 = f_1 \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right) = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Subtraction and simplification (2 marks):**
The Doppler shift is the difference $f_d = f_2 - f_0$:

$$f_d = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta - c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \right) = f_0 \left( \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Approximation and final form (1 mark):**
Since $v \ll c$ for blood flow, the denominator simplifies to $c$:

$$\boxed{f_d = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}}$$

**Assumptions (must be stated):**
- $v \ll c$ (non-relativistic)
- $\theta$ is the angle between beam and flow direction
- Reflector is a point scatterer (valid for RBC ensembles)
- Homogeneous medium of sound speed $c$

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

**Part (i) — True velocity (3 marks):**

$$v = \frac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0 \cos 45°} = \frac{800 \times 1540}{2 \times 3.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.7071}$$

$$v = \frac{1.232 \times 10^6}{4.95 \times 10^6} \approx 0.249 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{true} \approx 24.9 \text{ cm/s}}$$

**Part (ii) — Apparent velocity at wrong angle (4 marks):**

If operator assumes $\theta = 30°$:

$$v_{wrong} = \frac{800 \times 1540}{2 \times 3.5 \times 10^6 \times \cos 30°} = \frac{1.232 \times 10^6}{6.062 \times 10^6} \approx 0.203 \text{ m/s}$$

**Fractional error:**

$$\text{Error} = \frac{v_{true} - v_{wrong}}{v_{true}} = \frac{0.249 - 0.203}{0.249} \approx 0.184$$

$$\boxed{\text{Error} \approx 18.4\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. Forgetting to write the **assumptions** in the derivation part: **–1 mark**.
> 2. Mixing up the position of $\cos\theta$ (writing it in the numerator when rearranging): **–1 mark**.
> 3. In numerical problems, writing the unit in **cm/s** but dividing in **m/s** without conversion: **–0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 4. Not justifying why the denominator $c - v\cos\theta \approx c$ — examiners *always* expect this justification: **–0.5 mark**.
> 5. Using $c = 1500$ or $c = 340$ (speed in air) instead of **1540 m/s** for soft tissue: **–0.5 mark** (unless the problem specifies otherwise).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **pulse-echo principle** in detail and derive the relation between depth and time of flight. Discuss how A-mode, B-mode, and M-mode displays are obtained from the same principle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define acoustic impedance. Two media have acoustic impedances $Z_1 = 1.63 \times 10^6$ Rayl and $Z_2 = 1.70 \times 10^6$ Rayl respectively. Find: (i) the reflection coefficient, (ii) the transmission coefficient, and (iii) the percentage of incident intensity reflected. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

**Pulse-Echo Principle (3 marks):**
The transducer is excited by a short high-voltage pulse (typically 100–200 V, duration 0.5–2 µs). This pulse drives the piezoelectric crystal, converting electrical energy into an acoustic wave that propagates into the tissue. The crystal is then switched to a *receive* mode. Echoes returning from tissue interfaces excite the crystal, producing small voltages that are amplified, processed, and displayed.

**Depth Derivation (2 marks):**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

(As derived in Section 3.2 of these notes.)

**Display Modes (2 marks):**
- **A-mode:** The amplified echo signal is displayed as vertical spikes on a horizontal time-base. Spike height = echo amplitude; spike position = depth.
- **B-mode:** The amplitude signal modulates the **brightness** of a spot on the display, while the beam is scanned across the tissue to build a 2-D grayscale image.
- **M-mode:** A single B-mode line is displayed repeatedly as time advances, producing a "strip chart" of moving structures (e.g., heart valves).

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $Z_1 = 1.63 \times 10^6$ Rayl, $Z_2 = 1.70 \times 10^6$ Rayl.

**Part (i) — Reflection coefficient (2.5 marks):**

$$R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{1.70 - 1.63}{1.70 + 1.63} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{0.07}{3.33} \right)^2$$

$$R = (0.02102)^2 \approx 4.42 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.000442 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

**Part (ii) — Transmission coefficient (2.5 marks):**

$$T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = \frac{4 \times 1.63 \times 1.70}{(1.63 + 1.70)^2} = \frac{11.084}{11.0889} \approx 0.9996$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 0.99956}$$

**Part (iii) — Percentage of reflected intensity (2 marks):**

$$\text{Reflected \%} = R \times 100\% \approx 0.0442\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Approximately 0.044\% of the incident intensity is reflected.}}$$

*This very small reflection explains why ultrasound can image deep structures — most of the energy is transmitted forward.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Numerical Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Writing $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ (the **amplitude** reflection coefficient) instead of the **intensity** reflection coefficient with the square: this is a **1-mark penalty**.
> 2. Forgetting that $R + T = 1$ (a built-in self-check; if yours does not add to 1, you have an arithmetic error).
> 3. Mixing up $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ — since the formula uses $Z_2 - Z_1$, the result is the same after squaring, but sloppy work loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Summary — Master These Before Walking Into the KTU Exam:**

- [x] **Doppler Equation (memorize verbatim):** $f_d = \dfrac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}$
- [x] **Speed of sound in soft tissue:** $c = 1540$ m/s (NOT 340 m/s)
- [x] **Factor of 2 reason:** Round-trip motion — RBC is both observer and re-emitter.
- [x] **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$, in **Rayl**.
- [x] **Intensity reflection coefficient:** $R = \left( \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$
- [x] **Intensity transmission coefficient:** $T = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$
- [x] **Conservation law:** $R + T = 1$ (always!)
- [x] **Depth formula:** $d = \dfrac{c t}{2}$ (the 2 is for round trip).
- [x] **CW vs. PW Doppler:** CW has *no* depth discrimination; PW has *one* sample volume.
- [x] **Aliasing:** Occurs when $f_d > PRF/2$ (Nyquist limit).
- [x] **Display modes:** A = Amplitude spike, B = Brightness 2-D, M = Motion time-trace.
- [x] **Optimal Doppler angle:** $\theta \le 60°$ clinically; **90° = blind spot**.
- [x] **Attenuation rule of thumb:** $\alpha \approx 0.5$ dB/cm/MHz in soft tissue.
- [x] **Discovered by:** Christian Johann Doppler, **1842**.
- [x] **Common Examiner Trap:** Students forget the $\cos\theta$ in numericals and end up with velocities 2–3× too high.

**Mnemonic for modes — "A-B-M" = "Amplitude – Brightness – Motion"** — write this down first on your answer sheet to anchor your memory for the rest of the question.

**Golden Rule for KTU Derivations:** *Always state assumptions, always show the approximation $v \ll c$, and never skip a step. Examiners award method marks generously only if intermediate steps are visible.*

---

*End of Notes — Doppler Effect and Principle of Image Formation (Module 1, OEEBT723).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Details of US Probes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Constructional Details of US Probes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):** An **Ultrasound (US) Probe**, also called a **Ultrasound Transducer**, is an electro-acoustic device that performs bidirectional energy conversion between electrical energy and mechanical (acoustic) energy using the **piezoelectric effect**. In medical imaging, the probe acts as both the **transmitter** (converting high-frequency electrical pulses into longitudinal pressure waves in tissue) and the **receiver** (converting returning echoes into measurable electrical signals) — a phenomenon called the **pulse-echo principle**.

The active heart of every US probe is a thin slice of **piezoelectric ceramic**, most commonly **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)**. This material mechanically deforms when an alternating voltage is applied (the **converse piezoelectric effect**, discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880) and, conversely, generates a voltage when mechanically stressed (the **direct piezoelectric effect**).

The standard diagnostic medical ultrasound frequency range is **2 MHz to 15 MHz**, with specialized applications extending up to **40–50 MHz** (intravascular ultrasound / IVUS).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Drum Analogy":** Imagine a small, perfectly tuned drum with a skin made of a magic material. When you tap a voltage onto this drum, it vibrates and shouts an inaudible "ping" into your body. When that ping bounces back from an organ boundary, it gently taps the drum skin again, and the drum replies back with a tiny voltage we can measure. That magic skin is the **piezoelectric element**, and the drum-with-magic-skin is your **US probe**.

**Why both transmit AND receive?**
Because we need the same device to (a) push sound out and (b) listen for the echo. The probe is therefore a *transceiver*, not just a transmitter.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics Highlighted by KTU

- **Resonant Frequency ($f_r$):** the natural vibration frequency of the piezo disc, dictated by its thickness.
- **Bandwidth (BW):** the range of frequencies the probe can efficiently transmit/receive.
- **Q-Factor (Quality Factor):** a measure of how "sharp" or "damped" the resonance is.
- **Acoustic Impedance ($Z$):** resistance of a medium to sound wave propagation, $Z = \rho c$.
- **Wavelength ($\lambda$):** spatial period of the ultrasound wave, governing resolution.

These five parameters are **bolded as KTU-high-yield metrics** that appear in nearly every board exam question on this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of an Ultrasound Probe

A medical US probe is a precisely engineered **layered sandwich**. Each layer has one job, and the layers are bonded together with extreme precision (tolerances of a few micrometres).

### Constructional Components (in order from inside-out)

1. **Piezoelectric Element (Active Layer)**
   - Material: **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)** or **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride)** polymer for high-frequency applications.
   - Thickness typically **0.2 mm to 1 mm**.
   - Coated on both faces with thin **silver or nickel electrodes** for voltage application.
   - This is the only layer that *actually* generates/receives sound.

2. **Backing Material / Damping Block (Rear Layer)**
   - A heavy, acoustically absorptive composite (tungsten-epoxy, rubber, or metal-polymer matrix).
   - **Job:** Absorb backward-traveling acoustic energy to *shorten* the pulse → reduces **ring-down artifact**, increases **axial resolution**, and lowers the Q-factor.
   - High acoustic impedance (~$6$–$10 \times 10^6 \, \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$).

3. **Matching Layer(s) (Front Side, between piezo and tissue)**
   - One or more thin layers of material with acoustic impedance $Z_m$ between $Z_{\text{piezo}}$ and $Z_{\text{tissue}}$.
   - **Job:** Maximize transmission of acoustic energy from the high-$Z$ piezo into the low-$Z$ tissue by reducing the impedance mismatch. Without it, **>99% of the energy would be reflected back** at the piezo-tissue interface.
   - Optimal thickness $= \lambda/4$ at the operating frequency (quarter-wave transformer principle).

4. **Acoustic Lens (Elevation Focusing Element)**
   - A concave rubber or silicone lens bonded to the front of the matching layer.
   - **Job:** Focus the ultrasound beam in the **elevation (slice-thickness) plane** (perpendicular to the imaging plane). The array elements handle focusing in the lateral (azimuthal) plane electronically.

5. **Outer Wear Plate / Case**
   - Rigid plastic or metal housing providing mechanical and electrical shielding, waterproofing (most probes are IPX7-rated), and patient safety (electrical isolation).

6. **Cable & Connector**
   - Coaxial multi-conductor cable carrying the high-voltage excitation pulses (often ±50 to ±200 V) and returning microvolt-level echo signals.
   - Connects to the scanner's beamformer via a **multi-pin connector** (often 128–256 pins for modern arrays).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Layer — Engineering Logic

| Layer | Without it, what fails? | Design Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| Piezo element | No sound generated at all | None — essential |
| Backing | Long ring-down → poor axial resolution, blurred images | Too much damping → lower sensitivity |
| Matching layer | >99% energy reflected at tissue surface | Each added layer adds cost and complexity |
| Acoustic lens | Beam divergence → thick slice → poor elevation resolution | Fixed focal depth in elevation plane |
| Wear plate | Probe damages on patient contact | Must be acoustically transparent |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table — these equations appear in 80%+ of KTU questions on US probes.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resonant frequency of piezo disc | $f_r = \dfrac{v_p}{2d}$ | Hz (or MHz) | $v_p$ = velocity of sound in piezo; $d$ = thickness |
| 2 | Wavelength in tissue | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | m (or mm) | $c \approx 1540$ m/s in soft tissue |
| 3 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho \, c$ | $\text{Rayl} = \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$ | $\rho$ = density, $c$ = sound speed |
| 4 | Intensity transmission coefficient | $T = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | dimensionless | At normal incidence |
| 5 | Intensity reflection coefficient | $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Note: $R + T = 1$ |
| 6 | Matching layer impedance (single $\lambda/4$ layer) | $Z_m = \sqrt{Z_{\text{piezo}} \cdot Z_{\text{tissue}}}$ | Rayl | Quarter-wave transformer |
| 7 | Matching layer thickness | $t_m = \dfrac{\lambda_m}{4} = \dfrac{c_m}{4 f}$ | m | $c_m$ = sound speed in matching material |
| 8 | Q-factor (bandwidth measure) | $Q = \dfrac{f_r}{\Delta f} = \dfrac{f_r}{\text{BW}}$ | dimensionless | Low $Q$ → broadband, short pulses |
| 9 | Axial resolution | $\Delta z = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \text{BW}}$ | m | Pulse-echo factor of 2 included |
| 10 | Fractional bandwidth | $\text{FBW} = \dfrac{f_u - f_l}{f_c} \times 100\%$ | % | $f_c$ = centre frequency |
| 11 | Near field length (Fresnel zone) | $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4 \lambda} = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4 c}$ | m | $D$ = transducer diameter/aperture |
| 12 | Beam divergence angle (far field) | $\sin \theta = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$ | radians | For circular aperture |
| 13 | Beam intensity (free-field) | $I = \dfrac{P^2}{2 Z}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | $P$ = acoustic pressure amplitude |
| 14 | Attenuation in tissue (approx.) | $\alpha = \alpha_0 \, f^{n}$ | dB/cm | $n \approx 1$ (soft tissue), $f$ in MHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and pipe symbols above are written as `\vert` (in formulas) or omitted for clarity so as not to break the markdown table renderer. The vertical pipe `|` character is **forbidden** inside table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same layered sandwich design is used in:
- **SONAR** in submarines (low-frequency, large elements).
- **Industrial NDT** (non-destructive testing) of welds and aircraft components.
- **Piezoelectric inkjet printheads** in printers.
- **Quartz crystal microbalances** for chemical sensing.

The matching-layer principle (quarter-wave transformer) is identical to the optical **anti-reflection coating** on camera lenses — a direct analogy KTU examiners love to highlight.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resonant Frequency of the Piezoelectric Disc

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (Typical KTU 2-mark/7-mark setup):** Derive the expression for the thickness-mode resonant frequency of an ultrasound probe's piezoelectric element.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Standing Wave Condition

A piezoelectric disc vibrates in its **thickness mode** when an AC voltage is applied across its faces. The fundamental mechanical resonance occurs when the disc thickness equals exactly **half a wavelength** of the standing wave within the piezoelectric material.

$$
d = \frac{\lambda_p}{2}
$$

where $d$ is the disc thickness and $\lambda_p$ is the wavelength of sound inside the piezo material.

### Step 2 — Relate Wavelength to Velocity and Frequency

By the fundamental wave equation:
$$
v_p = f_r \cdot \lambda_p \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda_p = \frac{v_p}{f_r}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Into the Standing-Wave Condition

$$
d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{v_p}{f_r}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $f_r$

$$
f_r = \frac{v_p}{2 d}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Insight (KTU Examiner's Favourite):** This means **thinner elements vibrate at higher frequencies**. To build a 5 MHz probe, you need a PZT disc only **~400 µm thick** (using $v_p \approx 4000$ m/s for PZT). This is why high-frequency probes are extremely fragile and expensive.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quarter-Wave Matching Layer Impedance

### Step 1 — Define the Reflection Problem

Sound travels from a medium of impedance $Z_1$ into a medium of impedance $Z_2$. Without a matching layer, the reflected intensity is:

$$
R_0 = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2
$$

For a PZT-tissue interface: $Z_1 = Z_{\text{piezo}} \approx 30 \times 10^6$ Rayl, $Z_2 = Z_{\text{tissue}} \approx 1.6 \times 10^6$ Rayl.

### Step 2 — Compute the Unmatched Reflection

$$
R_0 = \left( \frac{1.6 - 30}{1.6 + 30} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{-28.4}{31.6} \right)^2 \approx 0.808
$$

So **~81% of acoustic energy would be reflected back into the piezo** — catastrophic for imaging. Only ~19% enters the tissue.

### Step 3 — Insert a $\lambda/4$ Matching Layer

The matching layer is placed *between* the two media, with impedance $Z_m$ and thickness $t_m = \lambda_m / 4$. We can use the transmission-line analogy (or, more rigorously, sum the two reflected waves with a 180° phase shift because the round-trip path is $\lambda/2$, producing destructive interference).

### Step 4 — Compute the Two Reflections

Reflection at the piezo–matching interface:
$$
r_1 = \frac{Z_m - Z_{\text{piezo}}}{Z_m + Z_{\text{piezo}}}
$$

Reflection at the matching–tissue interface:
$$
r_2 = \frac{Z_{\text{tissue}} - Z_m}{Z_{\text{tissue}} + Z_m}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply Destructive Interference
