# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Beam Steering Geometry

> **Goal:** Derive the relationship between inter-element time delay $\Delta\tau$ and steering angle $\theta_0$ for a uniform linear array.

Consider a 1-D linear array of $N$ elements, each of pitch $d$, aligned along the $x$-axis. We want the wavefront to be steered at angle $\theta_0$ measured from the array's *broadside* (perpendicular) direction.

**Step 1 — Wavefront Condition:** For constructive interference in direction $\theta_0$, the path difference between adjacent elements must equal an integer multiple of $\lambda$:

$$
d \sin\theta_0 = m\lambda
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Time:** The path difference is equivalent to a time difference of arrival. Since $c = \lambda f$, the corresponding delay between elements is:

$$
\Delta\tau = \frac{d \sin\theta_0}{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Steering Angle:**

$$
\sin\theta_0 = \frac{c\,\Delta\tau}{d}
$$

$$
\theta_0 = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{c\,\Delta\tau}{d}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Boundary for Physical Steering:** For the right-hand side to be $\le 1$ (real angle), we need:

$$
c\,\Delta\tau \le d
$$

This places a hard upper bound on $\Delta\tau$ for a given pitch.

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A linear array operates at $f_0 = 5\,\text{MHz}$ with pitch $d = 0.3\,\text{mm}$. The wavelength in tissue ($c = 1540\,\text{m/s}$) is:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^6} = 3.08 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 0.308\,\text{mm}
$$

Since $d = 0.3\,\text{mm} < \lambda = 0.308\,\text{mm}$, the array is properly sub-wavelength spaced (no grating lobes up to $\pm 90°$). For a steering angle of $\theta_0 = 30°$:

$$
\Delta\tau = \frac{d \sin 30°}{c} = \frac{0.3 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5}{1540} = 9.74 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{s} \approx 97.4\,\text{ns}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Focal Point Delay Calculation

> **Goal:** Derive the geometric delay required to focus the array at point $(x_f, z_f)$ in tissue.

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup:** Element $n$ lies at position $x_n = n d$ (taking element 0 as origin). The distance from element $n$ to the focal point is:

$$
R_n = \sqrt{(x_n - x_f)^2 + z_f^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Time-of-Flight for Element $n$:** The acoustic wave must travel this distance at speed $c$:

$$
t_n = \frac{R_n}{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize to the Most-Distant Element (or zero):** Conventionally, the delay is *applied* to the early-arriving element so that the central element is fired *last*. Choose the reference as the central element $n_0$ (closest to focus):

$$
\tau_n = t_n - t_{n_0} = \frac{1}{c}\left[R_n - z_f\right]
$$

since $R_{n_0} = z_f$ when the focus is on the central axis.

**Step 4 — Approximation for Far Focus ($z_f \gg N d$):** Using the Taylor expansion of $\sqrt{1 + \varepsilon} \approx 1 + \varepsilon/2$:

$$
R_n \approx z_f + \frac{(x_n - x_f)^2}{2 z_f}
$$

$$
\tau_n \approx \frac{(x_n - x_f)^2}{2 c\, z_f}
$$

> **This quadratic form is the cornerstone of all beamforming — it appears in the analytical matrix, the Fresnel approximation, and the *synthetic aperture* literature.**

**Step 5 — Combined Steering + Focus Delay:**

$$
\tau_n^{total} = \frac{1}{c}\left[R_n - z_f\right] - \frac{x_n \sin\theta_0}{c}
$$

The first term focuses; the second term steers. KTU examiners frequently ask students to compute this for given $N$, $d$, and focus coordinates.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Array Factor and Grating Lobes

> **Goal:** Show that the array factor has grating lobes when $d > \lambda$.

**Step 1 — Sum of Contributions:** For a uniform linear array (all elements equal amplitude, progressive phase $\beta = 2\pi d \sin\theta/\lambda$), the normalized array factor is:

$$
AF(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n \psi}
$$

where $\psi = kd(\sin\theta - \sin\theta_0) = \dfrac{2\pi d}{\lambda}(\sin\theta - \sin\theta_0)$.

**Step 2 — Closed-Form Summation (Geometric Series):**

$$
AF(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{jN\psi}}{1 - e^{j\psi}}
$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude (Dirichlet Kernel):**

$$
AF(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} = \frac{\sin\!\left[\dfrac{N\pi d}{\lambda}(\sin\theta - \sin\theta_0)\right]}{N\sin\!\left[\dfrac{\pi d}{\lambda}(\sin\theta - \sin\theta_0)\right]}
$$

**Step 4 — Grating Lobe Condition:** A grating lobe occurs wherever $AF(\theta) = 1$, i.e., when:

$$
\frac{\pi d}{\lambda}(\sin\theta - \sin\theta_0) = m\pi,\quad m = \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots
$$

$$
d(\sin\theta - \sin\theta_0) = m\lambda
$$

For $\theta_0 = 0$, this simplifies to:

$$
d \sin\theta_g = m\lambda \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sin\theta_g = \frac{m\lambda}{d}
$$

**Step 5 — Design Rule:** To keep the *first* grating lobe ($m = \pm 1$) outside the visible range $\vert\sin\theta_g\vert > 1$:

$$
\lambda > d
$$

> **Conclusion:** For an array that can be steered up to $\pm 90°$, the maximum allowable pitch is $d = \lambda$. KTU convention (and most textbooks) recommends $d = 0.5\lambda$ to $0.7\lambda$ to suppress grating lobes in the entire steering range, trading off against element sensitivity.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Steering & Focusing Trade-offs

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on Steering | Increase → Effect on Focus | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of elements $N$ | Narrower main lobe (better angular res.) | Tighter focus, narrower beam | More channels = higher cost |
| Pitch $d$ | Larger steering range possible | Worse grating-lobe margin | Limited by $\lambda$ |
| Frequency $f_0$ | Smaller $\lambda$ → smaller $d$ allowed | Better axial & lateral resolution | Higher attenuation in tissue |
| Steering angle $\theta_0$ | Beam shifts from broadside | Beam widens (cosine factor) | Steer only $\pm 30°$ typically |
| Focal depth $z_f$ | Constant | Smaller $z_f$ = tighter focus | Near-field beam diverges |
| Aperture $D = Nd$ | Constant | Wider aperture = better lateral res. | Larger probe footprint |

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Digital Beamforming Architecture for a Phased-Array System

> The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the high-level signal flow of a modern phased-array ultrasound system, mapping the functional interactions between the probe elements, the TX/RX beamformer, and the image reconstruction pipeline. This is the kind of system-level schematic a KTU 14-mark answer would expect.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ProbeAssembly["Phased Array Probe Assembly"]
        E1["Element 01"]
        E2["Element 02"]
        E3["Element 03"]
        E4["Element 04"]
        E5["Element N minus 1"]
        E6["Element N"]
    end

    subgraph TXSection["Transmit Beamformer Block"]
        DL["Programmable Delay Line Bank"]
        PULSER["High Voltage Pulser Matrix"]
        APOD_TX["TX Apodization Weight Block"]
    end

    subgraph RXSection["Receive Beamformer Block"]
        LNA["Low Noise Pre Amplifier"]
        TGC["Time Gain Compensation Block"]
        DL_RX["Dynamic Receive Delay Line"]
        APOD_RX["RX Apodization Weight Block"]
        SUM["Coherent Summation Block"]
    end

    subgraph ImageProcessing["Image Reconstruction Pipeline"]
        ENV["Envelope Detector"]
        LOGC["Log Compression"]
        SCAN["Scan Converter"]
        DISP["Display Memory"]
    end

    subgraph ControlUnit["System Control Unit"]
        CPU["Master Timing Sequencer"]
        FOC["Focus Delay Calculator"]
        STER["Steering Angle Calculator"]
    end

    CPU --> FOC
    CPU --> STER
    FOC --> DL
    FOC --> DL_RX
    STER --> DL
    STER --> DL_RX
    DL --> APOD_TX
    APOD_TX --> PULSER
    PULSER --> E1
    PULSER --> E2
    PULSER --> E3
    PULSER --> E4
    PULSER --> E5
    PULSER --> E6

    E1 --> LNA
    E2 --> LNA
    E3 --> LNA
    E4 --> LNA
    E5 --> LNA
    E6 --> LNA
    LNA --> TGC
    TGC --> DL_RX
    DL_RX --> APOD_RX
    APOD_RX --> SUM
    SUM --> ENV
    ENV --> LOGC
    LOGC --> SCAN
    SCAN --> DISP
    DISP --> CPU
```

> **How to read this diagram in an exam answer:**
> 1. **Transmit path (top-right to probe):** CPU computes delay and apodization weights → Delay Line → Pulser → Element.
> 2. **Receive path (probe to display):** Element → LNA → TGC → Receive Delay Line → Coherent Sum → Envelope → Log Compression → Scan Converter → Display.
> 3. **Feedback loop:** Display acquisition triggers CPU for the next firing sequence.
> 4. **Apodization** is applied on both TX and RX to suppress side-lobes (a key KTU 7-mark question topic).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define transducer array. Why are multi-element arrays preferred over single-element transducers in diagnostic ultrasound?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *transducer array* is an assembly of $N$ independent piezoelectric elements arranged in a defined geometric pattern (linear, annular, or 2-D), each with individually controllable excitation/reception delays, to form a single, dynamically reconfigurable acoustic beam.

Single-element transducers suffer from **fixed focus**, **no beam steering**, and **limited frame rate** (mechanical motion required). Multi-element arrays overcome these by:

1. **Electronic beam steering** — varying inter-element time delay $\Delta\tau$ to tilt the beam in real time.
2. **Dynamic focusing** — adjusting focus depth during receive to maintain uniform lateral resolution across the image.
3. **Higher frame rate** — all elements fire per pulse, no mechanical inertia.

*(Examiner distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for single-element limitation, 1 mark for the three multi-element advantages.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What are grating lobes? State the condition to suppress them in a linear array.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Grating lobes** are unwanted secondary maxima of the array's radiation pattern, occurring at angles other than the intended steering direction. They arise because the array spatially samples the acoustic field at discrete intervals.

**Condition for suppression:** The element pitch $d$ must satisfy:

$$
d \le \lambda
$$

Equivalently, the grating lobe angle $\theta_g$ must lie outside the visible range $\vert\sin\theta_g\vert \le 1$. For an unsteered array:

$$
\sin\theta_g = \frac{m\lambda}{d},\quad m = \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots
$$

Practical design uses $d = 0.5\lambda$ to $0.7\lambda$ to ensure suppression across the full steering range.

*(Examiner distribution: 1 mark for grating-lobe definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for design rule with $0.5\lambda$ to $0.7\lambda$ rationale.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Every Part B question is split into (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. The two internal-choice alternatives (A and B) cover *different* angles of the same module to test breadth of understanding.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **digital beamforming architecture** of a phased-array ultrasound system. Discuss the role of the *delay line bank*, *apodization*, and *time-gain compensation* (TGC) in image formation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 64-element linear array operating at 3.5 MHz in soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s), with element pitch $d = 0.44$ mm, compute:
- (i) The maximum steering angle without aliasing.
- (ii) The inter-element time delay required to steer the beam to 25° from broadside.
- (iii) The focal delay for element 32 to focus at a depth of 8 cm on the central axis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Block Diagram:** Present the same Mermaid-derived block diagram (TX beamformer, Probe, RX beamformer, Image Reconstruction, Control Unit). Describe each block in 1–2 lines.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Delay Line Bank:** A *programmable digital delay line* (typically with 8–12 bit resolution, $\sim$10–40 ns step) inserts calibrated time delays into each channel's signal. The delays are computed in real time by the *Focus Delay Calculator* for either TX or RX. On TX, the delays determine the wavefront tilt and focus; on RX, they align echoes before summation.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Apodization:** Apodization applies a *spatial weighting* (e.g., Hamming, Hanning, or Tukey window) across the array elements to taper the aperture amplitude. This **suppresses side-lobes** of the radiation pattern at the cost of a slightly wider main lobe. Typical apodization: 1.0 at center, ~0.3 at the edges.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — TGC:** *Time-Gain Compensation* is a depth-dependent amplification applied to received echoes to compensate for attenuation (typically 0.5 dB/cm/MHz one-way). It ensures that deep and shallow reflectors appear with similar brightness on the display. TGC is implemented as a swept gain curve, user-adjustable.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $N = 64$, $f = 3.5$ MHz, $c = 1540$ m/s, $d = 0.44$ mm, $\theta_0 = 25°$, $z_f = 8$ cm = 80 mm.

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Wavelength:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540}{3.5 \times 10^6} = 4.4 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 0.44\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Maximum Steering Angle:** For a real angle, $c\,\Delta\tau \le d$ implies $\sin\theta_{max} = 1$ (broadside limit), so $\theta_{max} = 90°$. However, since $d = \lambda$ (the Nyquist/spatial-Nyquist boundary), the *first grating lobe* would appear at broadside when steered to 90°. Hence, practical max steering:

$$
\theta_{max} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{d} \cdot 1 - 1\right) \approx 90°\text{ (theoretical)},\ \theta_{max}^{practical} \approx 70°\text{ to } 80°
$$

> **Most KTU examiners accept:** $\theta_{max} = 90°$ theoretical, but emphasize that $d = \lambda$ leaves no grating-lobe margin.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Inter-Element Delay for 25°:**

$$
\Delta\tau = \frac{d \sin\theta_0}{c} = \frac{0.44 \times 10^{-3} \times \sin 25°}{1540}
$$

$$
\Delta\tau = \frac{0.44 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4226}{1540} = \frac{1.859 \times 10^{-4}}{1540} = 1.207 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{s} = 120.7\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Focal Delay for Element 32:** Element 32 is on the central axis, so $x_{32} = 0$ mm and $x_f = 0$:

$$
\tau_{32} = \frac{1}{c}\left[\sqrt{(0 - 0)^2 + 80^2} - 80\right] = \frac{1}{c}[80 - 80] = 0\,\text{ns}
$$

> **This is the correct answer for the central element.** Markers must realize that on-axis elements require *zero* delay. (A common student error is to compute a non-zero value due to forgetting the reference normalization.)

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of **electronic beam steering** in a linear phased array. Derive the steering equation $\sin\theta_0 = c\,\Delta\tau / d$ and discuss the effect of increasing the steering angle on beam width. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **linear sequential arrays**, **linear phased arrays**, and **annular phased arrays** with respect to:
- (i) Field of view geometry
- (ii) Beam steering capability
- (iii) Focusing in the elevation plane
- (iv) Typical clinical applications

State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Principle of Steering:** In a linear array, beam steering is achieved by applying a **progressive time delay** across the elements. The element on one end of the array is fired *first*, the next is fired *slightly later*, and so on, such that the wavelets add constructively in a direction tilted from broadside.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Derivation:** See Section 3.1 above. Key equations to reproduce in the exam:

$$
d \sin\theta_0 = m\lambda \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sin\theta_0 = \frac{c\,\Delta\tau}{d}
$$

> **Acceptable form (KTU-standard):** $\theta_0 = \sin^{-1}(c\,\Delta\tau / d)$. The boundary condition $c\,\Delta\tau \le d$ must be stated for full credit.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Effect of Steering on Beam Width:** The effective aperture in the steering direction is reduced by a $\cos\theta_0$ factor (the *projected aperture* shrinks). Hence:

$$
BW(\theta_0) = \frac{BW_0}{\cos\theta_0}
$$

As $\theta_0$ increases, beam width increases (resolution degrades). At $\theta_0 = 60°$, beam width is doubled compared to broadside. This is a **fundamental geometric limit** — not a system error.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]**

**Comparison Table (1.75 marks per row × 4 rows = 7 marks):**

| Property | Linear Sequential | Linear Phased | Annular Phased |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) **Field of View** | Rectangular | Sector (pie-slice) | Sector (axisymmetric) |
| (ii) **Steering** | Only lateral translation, no steering | Full 2-D steering via delay | 2-D steering + symmetric focus |
| (iii) **Elevation Focus** | Fixed (lens) | Fixed (lens) | Dynamic, symmetric |
| (iv) **Clinical Use** | Vascular, MSK, breast | Cardiac, abdominal, transcranial | Cardiac (high-end), 3-D |

**Advantages and Disadvantages (0.25 marks each, total 1 mark):**
- **Linear Sequential:** Advantage: simple electronics. Disadvantage: no steering, slow frame rate.
- **Linear Phased:** Advantage: full steering. Disadvantage: small footprint limits aperture.
- **Annular Phased:** Advantage: symmetric focus in both planes. Disadvantage: mechanically complex rings, no 2-D matrix.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in this topic (and how to avoid them):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the reference normalization in focus delay** — students write $\tau_n = R_n/c$ instead of $\tau_n = (R_n - z_f)/c$. The latter is what the beamformer actually applies. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing $\lambda$ in tissue vs $\lambda$ in air** — always use $c = 1540$ m/s unless the problem states otherwise. Using $c = 340$ m/s in air for a medical probe is a hard fail.
> 3. **Not stating the grating-lobe condition** — many students write the formula $d \le \lambda$ but forget to *justify* it. Always add: "to keep the first grating lobe outside $\vert\sin\theta_g\vert \le 1$."
> 4. **Mixing up steering and focus in the combined delay** — these are additive, not multiplicative. The full TX delay is $\tau_n^{total} = \tau_n^{focus} - x_n \sin\theta_0/c$. A minus sign error will cost full marks on a derivation.
> 5. **Omitting the effect of steering on beam width** — examiners award a mark for noting that beam width scales as $1/\cos\theta_0$.
> 6. **Writing $d \le \lambda/2$ without context** — $\lambda/2$ is for *fully sampled* arrays; for steering up to 90°, $d = \lambda$ is sufficient. Pick the right rule and justify it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 30 minutes before the exam):**
>
> - **Transducer Array:** A set of $N$ phased piezoelectric elements producing a steerable, focusable acoustic beam.
> - **Pattern Multiplication Theorem:** $p(\theta) = H_e(\theta) \cdot AF(\theta)$. This is the master equation. Memorize it.
> - **Steering equation:** $\sin\theta_0 = c\,\Delta\tau / d$. Boundary: $c\,\Delta\tau \le d$.
> - **Focus delay:** $\tau_n = (R_n - z_f)/c$. Quadratic approximation: $\tau_n \approx (x_n - x_f)^2 / (2 c z_f)$.
> - **Array factor (ULA):** Dirichlet kernel. Main lobe at $\theta = \theta_0$. Grating lobes when $d \sin\theta_g = m\lambda$.
> - **Grating-lobe suppression rule:** $d \le \lambda$ for $\pm 90°$ steering. Practical: $d = 0.5\lambda$ to $0.7\lambda$.
> - **Beam width on steering:** $BW(\theta_0) = BW_0 / \cos\theta_0$. Resolution degrades off-broadside.
> - **Array types:** Linear sequential (translation only), Linear phased (steerable), Annular phased (symmetric), 2-D matrix (real-time 3-D).
> - **Apodization:** Spatial weighting to suppress side-lobes.
> - **TGC:** Depth-dependent amplification to compensate for attenuation.
> - **Standard values to remember:** $c = 1540$ m/s (tissue), $d_{max} = \lambda$ for steering, $d = 0.5\lambda$ for design margin.
> - **Key constants to bold in derivations:** $c = 1540$ m/s, $\lambda = c/f$, $d_{max} = \lambda$.
> - **Frame rate formula:** $FR = c / (2 N_{lines} z_{max})$ — useful for time-budgeting your answer.
> - **Examiner-favorite topics:** (1) derive steering equation, (2) compute focal delay for a given element, (3) explain grating-lobe condition, (4) compare array types, (5) sketch beamforming block diagram with labels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modes of Scanning - A Mode, B Mode, M Mode Scanners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modes of Scanning - A Mode, B Mode, M Mode Scanners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ultrasound Scanning — The Fundamental Principle

Medical ultrasound imaging is a **non-invasive, non-ionizing, real-time diagnostic modality** that uses high-frequency mechanical sound waves (typically **1 MHz to 20 MHz**) to visualize internal body structures. The three principal display modes — **A-mode, B-mode, and M-mode** — are differentiated by *how the returning echoes are processed and displayed* on the screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Scanning modes in diagnostic ultrasound refer to the specific methods by which reflected echo signals from tissue interfaces are processed, mapped, and visually presented to extract anatomical or functional information. The three classical modes — Amplitude (A), Brightness (B), and Motion (M) — form the foundational display architecture upon which all modern 2D, 3D, and Doppler ultrasound systems are built.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Echo in a Dark Cave"

Imagine you are standing in a long, dark cave shouting *"Hello!"* and listening for the echo:
- The **louder the echo**, the bigger the wall in front of you.
- The **longer it takes** for the echo to return, the **farther** the wall.
- If you move your head from left to right while shouting, you can **map the cave's shape** (a 2D picture).
- If you keep your head still but shout repeatedly and **watch the timing of echoes change**, you can detect something **moving** (like a swinging pendulum).

This is *exactly* how ultrasound works:
| Real-world Analogy | Ultrasound Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Shouting into the cave | Transducer emitting a sound pulse |
| Listening for the echo | Transducer receiving reflected echo |
| Loudness of echo | Echo amplitude (A-mode) |
| Position of echo (time delay) | Depth of reflecting tissue |
| Moving head side-to-side | B-mode scanning (2D image) |
| Watching echoes over time | M-mode (motion display) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constant to Memorize:**
> Average velocity of ultrasound in soft tissue = **c = 1540 m/s** (≈ 1.54 mm/µs).
> This value is universally used in all KTU numerical problems.

### 1.3 The Three Modes at a Glance

| Mode | Full Form | Display Type | Primary Use | Dimension |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A-mode** | Amplitude Mode | Spike/Deflection display | Biometry, echoencephalography | 1-D |
| **B-mode** | Brightness Mode | Grayscale 2D image | General anatomical imaging | 2-D |
| **M-mode** | Motion Mode | Time-motion strip | Cardiac valve motion | 1-D + time |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 A-Mode (Amplitude Mode) Scanner — The "One-Dimensional Spike"

A-mode is the **oldest and simplest** form of ultrasound display. The transducer is held **stationary** at one position, and a single pulse is sent along a single line of sight.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. A short electrical pulse excites the piezoelectric crystal → produces an ultrasound pulse.
2. The pulse travels into tissue; partial reflections occur at acoustic impedance mismatches.
3. The returning echoes are detected by the same transducer (pulse-echo principle).
4. The **vertical deflection (amplitude)** of the displayed spike is proportional to the **strength of the echo**.
5. The **horizontal position (x-axis)** of the spike represents **time-of-flight**, which is converted to **depth** using:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

where:
- $d$ = depth of reflecting interface (m)
- $c$ = velocity of sound in tissue (**1540 m/s**)
- $t$ = round-trip time of the echo (s)
- The factor **2** accounts for the *round trip* (down and back up).

**Why A-mode is still relevant:**
- Used in **ophthalmology** to measure axial length of the eye.
- Used in **echoencephalography** to detect midline shift in the brain.
- Provides the **highest precision one-dimensional measurement** of tissue boundaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of the factor 2:**
> The clock measures the *total* time for the sound to travel *to* the target AND *back*. Since we only want the *one-way* distance, we must divide the total path length by 2.

### 2.2 B-Mode (Brightness Mode) Scanner — The "2D Picture"

B-mode converts echo amplitude into **brightness** rather than vertical deflection. A 2D image is built by sweeping the ultrasound beam across the tissue (mechanically or electronically).

**Operational Logic:**
1. The transducer emits pulses along **multiple scan lines** (e.g., 128, 256 lines per frame).
2. For each scan line, the returning echoes are displayed as **bright dots** along that line — dot brightness $\propto$ echo amplitude.
3. Multiple adjacent scan lines are placed side-by-side to form a **2D cross-sectional image**.
4. The frame is refreshed many times per second (**real-time imaging**, typically 15–60 frames/sec).

**Two B-mode scanning architectures:**
- **Mechanical sector scanner:** A single transducer oscillates or rotates (used in early systems).
- **Electronic phased array:** Multiple crystal elements are fired with **precise time delays** to steer and focus the beam electronically — this is the dominant modern technology.

**The "Why" behind brightness mapping:**
The human eye is *much better* at distinguishing shades of gray than tiny height differences on an oscilloscope. B-mode leverages this to create an **anatomically intuitive image**.

### 2.3 M-Mode (Motion Mode) Scanner — "Time on the X-axis"

M-mode is essentially A-mode data **plotted continuously over time**. The transducer is held stationary, and a single scan line is repeatedly interrogated. Each successive A-mode trace is drawn slightly to the right of the previous one.

**Operational Logic:**
1. A-mode echoes are acquired repeatedly (e.g., **1000 times/second**).
2. The x-axis represents **time** (not depth).
3. The y-axis represents **depth** (just like A-mode).
4. Echo amplitude is converted to **brightness** (like B-mode).
5. The result: a **strip-chart image** where moving structures (e.g., heart valves) trace wavy patterns, and stationary structures appear as **straight horizontal lines**.

**Primary Clinical Application:** **Echocardiography** — measuring valve motion, chamber wall thickening/thinning, and ejection fraction.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Depth from echo time | m, s | A, B, M modes |
| 2 | $t = \dfrac{2d}{c}$ | Time-of-flight | s | All modes |
| 3 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Frequency of pulse | Hz | M-mode PRF |
| 4 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Acoustic impedance | kg/(m²·s) = Rayl | Reflection physics |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{(Z_2 - Z_1)^2}{(Z_2 + Z_1)^2}$ | Reflection coefficient (intensity) | dimensionless | Echo formation |
| 6 | $d_{axial} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$ | Axial resolution | m | B-mode |
| 7 | $\theta = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{d}\right)$ | Beam divergence half-angle | radians | A/B mode |
| 8 | $N = \dfrac{FOV_{depth}}{d_{axial}}$ | No. of samples per scan line | — | A-mode |
| 9 | $f_{frame} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot N \cdot L}$ | Max frame rate (B-mode) | Hz | B-mode |
| 10 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_0}$ | Wavelength | m | All modes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the constant:** $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$ in soft tissue. In **fat** $c \approx 1450$ m/s; in **bone** $c \approx 4080$ m/s; in **blood** $c \approx 1570$ m/s. The KTU board exam almost always uses 1540 m/s unless specified.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **A-mode** → foundation for biometric measurements and tissue characterization.
- **B-mode** → the workhorse of radiology, obstetrics, and emergency medicine.
- **M-mode** → the gold standard for time-resolved cardiac motion analysis with **temporal resolution in milliseconds** (much higher than 2D B-mode).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental Pulse-Echo Depth Equation

**Statement to Prove:** The depth of a reflecting interface is given by $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$.

**Starting Premise:** Ultrasound travels at a constant velocity $c$ through a uniform medium.

**Step 1 — Define one-way travel:**
The time taken for the sound to travel *from* the transducer *to* the reflecting interface is:

$$t_1 = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 2 — Define return travel:**
The echo travels back from the interface to the transducer, covering the *same* distance $d$ at the *same* speed $c$:

$$t_2 = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Total round-trip time:**
The total measured time $t$ is the sum of the two one-way trips:

$$t = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{d}{c} + \frac{d}{c} = \frac{2d}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for depth $d$:**
Rearranging the equation for $d$:

$$2d = c \cdot t$$

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Q.E.D.** This is the **single most important equation in diagnostic ultrasound.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Numerical Example — Depth of a Liver Interface

**Problem:** An A-mode ultrasound system emits a pulse that returns an echo **26 µs** after emission. Calculate the depth of the reflecting interface. Assume $c = 1540$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**
- $t = 26 \, \mu s = 26 \times 10^{-6} \, s$
- $c = 1540 \, m/s$

**Step 2 — Apply the depth formula:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{1540 \times 26 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$1540 \times 26 = 40040$$

$$40040 \times 10^{-6} = 0.040040$$

**Step 4 — Divide by 2:**

$$d = \frac{0.040040}{2} = 0.020020 \, m$$

**Step 5 — Convert to cm:**

$$d \approx 2.00 \, cm$$

**Answer:** The reflecting interface is at a depth of **2 cm**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Wavelength and Axial Resolution

**Statement:** Axial resolution $d_{axial}$ is inversely proportional to transducer bandwidth $\Delta f$.

**Step 1 — Axial resolution is the minimum distance along the beam axis that the system can distinguish.** It is determined by the **spatial pulse length (SPL)** of the emitted wave:

$$\text{SPL} = n \cdot \lambda$$

where $n$ = number of cycles in the pulse, and $\lambda$ = wavelength.

**Step 2 — Wavelength from frequency:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0}$$

**Step 3 — A short pulse (high axial resolution) corresponds to a broadband frequency spectrum.** For a Gaussian pulse, the relationship between spatial pulse length and bandwidth is:

$$\text{SPL} = \frac{c}{\Delta f}$$

**Step 4 — Two echoes can be resolved only if they are separated by at least SPL/2 (Rayleigh criterion).** Therefore:

$$d_{axial} = \frac{\text{SPL}}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$$

**Numerical Example:** If $\Delta f = 2 \, \text{MHz}$ and $c = 1540$ m/s:

$$d_{axial} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 2 \times 10^6} = \frac{1540}{4 \times 10^6}$$

$$d_{axial} = 3.85 \times 10^{-4} \, m = 0.385 \, mm$$

This means the system can resolve two interfaces separated by as little as **0.385 mm** along the beam axis.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — A vs B vs M Mode

| Parameter | A-Mode | B-Mode | M-Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Display axis X** | Time (→ depth) | Lateral position | Time |
| **Display axis Y** | Amplitude (voltage) | Depth | Depth |
| **Echo representation** | Spike height | Pixel brightness | Pixel brightness |
| **No. of scan lines** | 1 (single line) | Many (e.g., 128–256) | 1 (single line, repeated) |
| **Transducer motion** | Stationary | Sweeping (mech./electronic) | Stationary |
| **Image dimension** | 1-D | 2-D | 1-D + time (2-D plot) |
| **Temporal resolution** | N/A | Limited by frame rate | Very high (~ms) |
| **Clinical use** | Biometry, eye | General imaging | Cardiac motion |
| **Data density** | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| **Origin of mode** | 1950s (Wild, Howry) | 1950s (Howry, Bliss) | 1950s–60s (Elder, Hertz) |

### 3.5 Derivation 4: M-Mode Cardiac Wall Velocity

**Problem:** An M-mode trace shows that a heart valve echo moves from depth 4.0 cm to 4.6 cm over an interval of 0.5 seconds. Calculate the average velocity of the valve.

**Step 1 — Compute displacement:**

$$\Delta d = 4.6 - 4.0 = 0.6 \, cm = 6 \times 10^{-3} \, m$$

**Step 2 — Apply velocity formula:**

$$v = \frac{\Delta d}{\Delta t} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$v = 12 \times 10^{-3} \, m/s = 12 \, mm/s$$

**Answer:** The valve moves at an average velocity of **12 mm/s** during the observation interval.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Common Signal Chain for A/B/M-Mode Scanners

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pulser / Transmitter] --> B[Piezoelectric Transducer]
    B --> C[Patient Tissue Interfaces]
    C --> D[Returning Echoes]
    D --> B
    B --> E[T/R Switch and Preamplifier]
    E --> F[Time Gain Compensation TGC]
    F --> G[Envelope Detector and Log Compression]
    G --> H{Mode Selector}
    H -->|A Mode| I1[Spike Amplitude on Time Base]
    H -->|B Mode| I2[Pixel Brightness with Scan Converter]
    H -->|M Mode| I3[Strip Chart Time vs Depth]
    I1 --> J[Display Monitor]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Mode Differentiation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PULSE[Ultrasound Pulse Generated] --> X{Transducer Motion?}
    X -->|Stationary Single Line| Y{Echo Display Format}
    X -->|Swept Multiple Lines| BMODE[B-Mode 2D Image]
    Y -->|Amplitude Deflection| AMODE[A-Mode 1D Spikes]
    Y -->|Brightness vs Time| MMODE[M-Mode Strip Chart]
    AMODE --> APP1[Ophthalmology Biometry]
    BMODE --> APP2[Obstetrics Abdomen Vascular]
    MMODE --> APP3[Echocardiography Cardiac]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — M-Mode Signal Acquisition Timing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Time t=0 Emit Pulse 1] --> T1[Wait for Echo Round Trip]
    T1 --> T2[Display Column 1 Brightness vs Depth]
    T2 --> T3[Time t=Tprf Emit Pulse 2]
    T3 --> T4[Display Column 2 Next to Column 1]
    T4 --> T5[Time t=2Tprf Emit Pulse 3]
    T5 --> T6[Display Column 3 Next to Column 2]
    T6 --> T7[Repeat at PRF typically 1000 Hz]
```

### 4.4 A-Mode Spike Display Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[Transducer on Skin Surface] --> SKIN[Skin Interface Echo at t1]
    SKIN --> FAT[Fat Tissue Echo at t2]
    FAT --> LIVER[Liver Interface Echo at t3]
    LIVER --> LESION[Lesion Boundary Echo at t4]
    LESION --> POST[Posterior Wall Echo at t5]
    POST --> SCREEN[Oscilloscope Display with Spikes at t1 t2 t3 t4 t5]
```

### 4.5 B-Mode Image Formation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LINES[Multiple Adjacent Scan Lines] --> LINES1[Scan Line 1]
    LINES --> LINES2[Scan Line 2]
    LINES --> LINES3[Scan Line N]
    LINES1 --> BRIGHT1[Brightness Profile along Line 1]
    LINES2 --> BRIGHT2[Brightness Profile along Line 2]
    LINES3 --> BRIGHT3[Brightness Profile along Line N]
    BRIGHT1 --> MERGE[2D Pixel Matrix Assembly]
    BRIGHT2 --> MERGE
    BRIGHT3 --> MERGE
    MERGE --> FRAME[Complete 2D Frame Image]
    FRAME --> REFRESH[Refreshing at 15 to 60 Frames per Second]
```

### 4.6 M-Mode Time-Depth Trace Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MTRACE
    A1[Time t0 Stationary Echo] --> A2[Time t1 Echo Moves Up]
    A2 --> A3[Time t2 Echo Returns Down]
    A3 --> A4[Time t3 Echo at Mid Position]
    A4 --> A5[Time t4 Echo Moves Up Again]
    end
    MTRACE --> INTERP[Pattern of Wavy Lines Indicates Moving Structure]
    INTERP --> CLIN[Clinical Reading: Valve Opening and Closing]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an A-mode ultrasound scanner. Mention its clinical applications.*

**Model Answer (3-mark structure):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A-mode (Amplitude mode) is a one-dimensional pulse-echo ultrasound display in which the *vertical deflection* (amplitude) of a displayed spike is proportional to the strength of the echo returning from a tissue interface, and the *horizontal position* of the spike corresponds to the time-of-flight (depth) of that echo.

**Block Diagram + Operation (1.5 marks):**
The pulser excites the piezoelectric transducer, which emits a short ultrasound pulse into the body. Returning echoes are detected by the same transducer, routed through a T/R switch, amplified with Time Gain Compensation (TGC), and displayed on an oscilloscope. The x-axis is swept at a calibrated time-base such that distance = (c × t) / 2, with c = 1540 m/s.

**Applications (0.5 mark):**
Used in ophthalmology for axial length measurement of the eye, echoencephalography for midline shift detection, and as the foundational measurement mode for all other ultrasound techniques.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between B-mode and M-mode ultrasound displays. Give one clinical application of each.*

**Model Answer (3-mark structure):**

| Feature | B-Mode | M-Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Display | 2D grayscale image | Time-depth strip chart |
| Transducer | Sweeps multiple lines | Held stationary |
| Axis | X = lateral position, Y = depth | X = time, Y = depth |
| Clinical use | General anatomy (abdomen, obstetrics) | Cardiac valve motion |

**B-mode application (0.5 mark):** Obstetric scanning — visualizing the fetus.
**M-mode application (0.5 mark):** Echocardiography — measuring mitral valve excursion.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — A-Mode and B-Mode Deep Dive**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of an A-mode ultrasound scanner. Explain how the depth of a reflecting interface is determined from the time-of-flight of the echo. (7 marks)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*Explain the working principle of a B-mode scanner with a block diagram. Compare its performance with A-mode in terms of image dimension, clinical utility, and information content. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) Model Answer:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
The A-mode scanner consists of:
- **Pulser (transmitter):** Generates short high-voltage electrical pulses (typically 100–400 V, nanosecond duration).
- **Piezoelectric transducer:** Single-element crystal (e.g., lead zirconate titanate, PZT) that converts electrical pulses to ultrasound and vice versa.
- **T/R (Transmit/Receive) switch:** Protects the receiver during transmission; routes returning echoes to the receiver.
- **RF amplifier with TGC:** Compensates for attenuation with depth by progressively increasing gain with time.
- **Display:** CRT or LCD oscilloscope with calibrated time base.

**Working (3 marks):**
The pulser fires a short electrical pulse → transducer emits an ultrasound pulse → pulse travels into tissue → at each acoustic impedance boundary a portion of the energy is reflected → returning echo strikes the transducer → transducer converts it back to electrical signal → signal is amplified (with TGC) and displayed on the oscilloscope. The oscilloscope's horizontal sweep is triggered at the moment of pulse emission. Each echo produces a vertical spike whose **height is proportional to the echo amplitude** and whose **horizontal position is proportional to the round-trip time**.

**Depth Calculation (2 marks):**
Depth is computed from round-trip time $t$ using:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

with $c = 1540$ m/s. The factor of 2 divides the total round-trip distance by 2 to obtain the one-way depth.

**Part (b) Model Answer:**

**Working Principle of B-mode (4 marks):**
The B-mode scanner builds a **2D grayscale image** by sweeping the ultrasound beam across the tissue. For each of many adjacent scan lines (e.g., 128 or 256), the system acquires A-mode-like echo data. However, instead of displaying the echo amplitude as a spike height, the system maps it to **pixel brightness** (grayscale). The pixels from all scan lines are then assembled in a 2D array (scan converter memory) to form a complete cross-sectional image. Modern systems use **electronic phased arrays** where multiple crystal elements are fired with time delays to steer the beam, eliminating mechanical motion.

The frame is refreshed continuously at 15–60 frames/second to provide **real-time imaging**.

**Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | A-Mode | B-Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Image dimension | 1-D (single line) | 2-D (cross-section) |
| Echo display | Spike amplitude | Pixel brightness |
| Information content | Depth + amplitude only | Spatial map of tissue |
| Clinical utility | Biometry, simple measurements | Full anatomical imaging |
| Real-time capability | Not applicable | Yes (real-time) |
| Complexity | Low | High (requires scan converter) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — M-Mode and Multi-Mode Comparison**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Adapted]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*Explain the working of an M-mode ultrasound scanner with a suitable block diagram. How is it different from A-mode and B-mode scanners? Discuss its clinical importance in echocardiography. (7 marks)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*A B-mode ultrasound system uses a transducer with center frequency 5 MHz and bandwidth 3 MHz. Calculate (i) the axial resolution, (ii) the wavelength in tissue, and (iii) the depth of an interface that produces an echo 39 µs after emission. Assume c = 1540 m/s. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) Model Answer:**

**Working of M-mode (3 marks):**
In M-mode, the transducer is held **stationary** and a **single scan line** is repeatedly interrogated at a high **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)**, typically ~1000 Hz. The echoes are processed exactly as in A-mode (TGC, envelope detection) but are displayed as **brightness values (not spike heights)** along a vertical depth axis. Each successive A-mode trace is plotted slightly to the right of the previous one, with the horizontal axis now representing **time**. The result is a **strip-chart image** where:
- Stationary structures → straight horizontal lines.
- Moving structures (e.g., heart valves) → wavy lines whose pattern reveals the motion.

**Differences from A-mode and B-mode (2 marks):**

| Mode | A | B | M |
|---|---|---|---|
| Display | Spike amplitude | 2D image | Time-depth strip |
| Motion of transducer | Stationary | Sweeping | Stationary |
| Time axis | No (only depth) | No (only spatial) | Yes |

**Clinical Importance in Echocardiography (2 marks):**
M-mode provides **extremely high temporal resolution** (1 ms), which is essential for visualizing rapidly moving cardiac structures like valve leaflets, chamber walls, and the pericardium. It enables accurate measurement of:
- Mitral valve E-F slope (anterior leaflet motion).
- Left ventricular internal dimensions during systole and diastole.
- Pericardial effusion detection.
- Ejection fraction estimation.

**Part (b) Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Center frequency $f_0 = 5 \, \text{MHz} = 5 \times 10^6 \, \text{Hz}$
- Bandwidth $\Delta f = 3 \, \text{MHz} = 3 \times 10^6 \, \text{Hz}$
- Velocity of ultrasound $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$
- Echo time $t = 39 \, \mu s = 39 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{s}$

**(i) Axial Resolution (2 marks):**

$$d_{axial} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 3 \times 10^6}$$

$$d_{axial} = \frac{1540}{6 \times 10^6} = 2.567 \times 10^{-4} \, m$$

$$\boxed{d_{axial} \approx 0.257 \, mm}$$

**(ii) Wavelength (2 marks):**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^6}$$

$$\lambda = 308 \times 10^{-6} \, m = 0.308 \, mm$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 0.308 \, mm}$$

**(iii) Depth of Interface (3 marks):**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{1540 \times 39 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{60060 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.06006}{2}$$

$$d = 0.03003 \, m = 3.003 \, cm$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 3 \, cm}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the depth formula: students often write $d = c \cdot t$ instead of $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$. This is the **single most common error** in ultrasound numericals and will cost **full marks** in that sub-part.
> 2. **Unit conversion mistakes:** converting µs to seconds (×10⁻⁶) and m/s to mm/µs (×10⁻³/10⁻⁶ = divide by 1000 to get mm) is where most students slip.
> 3. **Confusing axial with lateral resolution:** axial resolution uses *bandwidth*; lateral resolution uses *beam width* — do not mix them.
> 4. **In A-mode questions**, students forget to draw the block diagram with **T/R switch, TGC, and time-base** — at least **1 mark is reserved** for a labeled diagram.
> 5. **In M-mode questions**, students often describe it as a "2D image" — it is **NOT 2D**; it is a 1-D depth display varying with time. This is a **favourite examiner trap**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **A-mode** → 1-D display; spike height = amplitude; x-axis = depth (via time).
- [x] **B-mode** → 2-D grayscale image; pixel brightness = echo amplitude; built from many scan lines.
- [x] **M-mode** → 1-D depth + time strip; transducer stationary; uses A-mode data plotted as brightness.
- [x] **Master equation:** $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ with **c = 1540 m/s** in soft tissue.
- [x] **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho \cdot c$ (Rayl); echo occurs when $Z_1 \neq Z_2$.
- [x] **Reflection coefficient (intensity):** $R = \dfrac{(Z_2 - Z_1)^2}{(Z_2 + Z_1)^2}$.
- [x] **Axial resolution:** $d_{axial} = \dfrac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ — broadband transducer = better resolution.
- [x] **Lateral resolution:** determined by beam width (focused at depth of interest).
- [x] **Wavelength:** $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_0}$.
- [x] **Round-trip time** → always remember the factor of 2.
- [x] **A-mode clinical use:** ophthalmology biometry, echoencephalography.
- [x] **B-mode clinical use:** general radiology, obstetrics, abdominal imaging.
- [x] **M-mode clinical use:** echocardiography (valve motion, wall thickness).
- [x] **Piezoelectric effect:** reciprocity — same crystal transmits and receives.
- [x] **PRF (Pulse Repetition Frequency):** number of pulses per second per scan line.
- [x] **Frame rate in B-mode:** depends on depth and number of scan lines — deeper = slower.
- [x] **Modern B-mode systems use phased-array transducers** with electronic beam steering.
- [x] **Attenuation** in tissue increases with frequency — limits deep penetration at high MHz.
- [x] **Higher frequency = better resolution** but **shallower penetration** (fundamental trade-off).
- [x] **TGC (Time Gain Compensation):** amplifies echoes from deeper tissues to compensate for attenuation.
- [x] **All three modes share the same front-end** (pulser, transducer, T/R switch, receiver) — only the *display processing* differs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Duplex Scanning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Duplex Scanning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duplex Scanning (Duplex Ultrasonography)** is a non-invasive diagnostic imaging modality that simultaneously combines **real-time B-mode (Brightness-mode) grayscale anatomical imaging** with **pulsed-wave (PW) Doppler flow analysis** to provide a complete, spatially registered evaluation of vascular structures, their surrounding tissues, and the hemodynamic properties of blood flowing within them.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, Duplex Scanning represents the foundational clinical fusion of *structural imaging* and *functional flow assessment* within a single transducer head and a unified display interface.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Traffic Helicopter Reporter"**
> Imagine a traffic helicopter hovering over a busy highway. The downward camera (B-mode) shows you **where the cars are**, how many lanes exist, and any road obstacles. At the same instant, the radio microphone (Doppler) tells you **how fast each car is moving** and **in which direction**. A duplex scanner does exactly this for blood inside an artery: the **B-mode image** is the "helicopter camera" mapping the vessel wall, lumen, and surrounding tissues, while the **Doppler spectral display** acts like the "microphone" reporting blood cell velocity and direction at a chosen sample volume.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard clinical thresholds govern duplex scanning:

- **Speed of ultrasound in soft tissue (c):** $c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$ (industry standard assumption)
- **Speed of ultrasound in blood:** $\approx 1570 \text{ m/s}$
- **Standard Doppler transmit frequencies:** $2 \text{ MHz}$, $3.5 \text{ MHz}$, $5 \text{ MHz}$, $7.5 \text{ MHz}$, $10 \text{ MHz}$
- **Diagnostic ultrasound intensity limit (FDA):** $I_{SPTA} \le 720 \text{ mW/cm}^2$
- **Aliasing velocity limit:** $V_{max} = \frac{c^2}{8 \cdot f_0 \cdot D_{depth}}$ (where $D_{depth}$ is transducer depth)
- **Frame rate (B-mode):** typically **15 – 60 frames/second**
- **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF):** **1 kHz – 25 kHz** depending on depth
- **Color Doppler frame rate:** **8 – 30 frames/second**

## 1.4 What Duplex Scanning is NOT

> [!CAUTION]
> Duplex scanning is often confused with related but distinct techniques. Clarify:
> - **Duplex** = B-mode **+** Spectral PW Doppler (on same image, share screen).
> - **Triplex (Color Duplex)** = B-mode **+** Color Doppler **+** Spectral Doppler.
> - **Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler alone** is *not* duplex — it lacks the 2D B-mode image.
> - **Color Doppler alone** is *not* duplex — it is the 2D flow map *without* the quantitative spectral waveform.

---

## 1.5 The Two Sub-Modalities of Duplex Scanning

| Sub-Type | Doppler Mode Used | Range-Gating | Clinical Use |
|----------|-------------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Continuous-Wave (CW) Duplex** | Two crystals — one continuously transmitting, one continuously receiving | No range resolution; samples the entire beam path | High-velocity flows (e.g., valvular stenosis, regurgitation) where aliasing in PW is a problem |
| **Pulsed-Wave (PW) Duplex** | Single crystal pulses, gates a specific "sample volume" | Yes — operator places sample volume on a specific anatomical point on the B-mode image | Standard peripheral vascular, carotid, venous, and abdominal studies |

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently frames exam questions around *why PW Doppler is preferred for most duplex applications* — the answer is always the **range resolution / sample volume placement** capability enabled by the B-mode anatomical reference.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of a Duplex Scanner

A duplex scanner is a sophisticated **time-shared, multi-mode signal processing system**. The same piezoelectric transducer array is multiplexed in time between two distinct operating regimes:

### Stage 1 — B-Mode Image Formation (Anatomical Map)
1. The scanner transmits a **short, high-amplitude, broadband pulse** (typically 2 – 4 cycles) into tissue.
2. Echoes return from acoustic impedance mismatches at tissue interfaces.
3. The time-of-flight $t = 2d/c$ (where $d$ is depth, $c$ is speed of sound) determines the axial position.
4. The amplitude of returning echoes is mapped to a grayscale pixel value (envelope detection + log compression).
5. Electronic beamforming across the array produces a 2D sector or linear image at **15 – 60 fps**.

### Stage 2 — Doppler Flow Sampling (Hemodynamic Function)
1. The operator places a **sample volume gate** (typically 1 – 15 mm in length) at a specific (x, y) location on the B-mode image.
2. The scanner switches to PW Doppler mode, transmitting **longer, narrow-band pulses** at a Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF).
3. Returning echoes from the sample volume are mixed with a reference oscillator and quadrature-demodulated to extract the **Doppler shift frequency** $f_D$.
4. A **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** or auto-correlation processor converts the time-domain Doppler signal into a **spectral waveform** displayed below the B-mode image in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "duplex" name comes from this **simultaneous, time-shared dual acquisition** — anatomical and functional data are co-registered in real time on the same screen.

### Stage 3 — Display Integration
- **Top half of screen:** Real-time B-mode grayscale image with the sample volume marker overlaid.
- **Bottom half of screen:** Time-velocity spectral waveform (X-axis = time, Y-axis = Doppler shift frequency or velocity).
- **Audio output:** The Doppler shift is also fed to a stereo speaker, allowing the sonographer to "hear" the flow characteristics (a unique clinical advantage).

## 2.2 The Core Physical Principle — The Doppler Effect

When an ultrasound wave of frequency $f_0$ strikes a moving red blood cell (RBC) acting as a scatterer, the reflected wave undergoes a **frequency shift** $f_D$ governed by the Doppler equation:

$$\boxed{f_D = \frac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}}$$

Where:
- $f_D$ = Doppler shift frequency (Hz)
- $f_0$ = Transmitted ultrasound frequency (Hz)
- $v$ = Velocity of the RBC scatterer (m/s)
- $\theta$ = Angle between ultrasound beam axis and direction of blood flow (degrees)
- $c$ = Speed of sound in tissue (≈ 1540 m/s)

The factor of **2** arises because Doppler shift occurs **twice** — once when the wave is incident on the moving RBC, and again when the reflected wave is received by the (now effectively stationary) transducer.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-essentials** set. Memorize the units, the limit conditions, and the cosine-theta dependence — these are the most-tested items.

| # | Formula / Relationship | LaTeX Form | Units / Limit | Engineering Utility |
|---|------------------------|------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Doppler shift frequency | $f_D = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | Hz; $f_D$ (audio) is **20 Hz – 20 kHz** | The master equation — derives all velocity measurements |
| 2 | Resolved velocity (rearranged) | $v = \dfrac{c \cdot f_D}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | m/s | Direct clinical velocity output |
| 3 | Nyquist limit (max unambiguous velocity) | $v_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot PRF}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | m/s | Sets the **aliasing threshold** for PW Doppler |
| 4 | Maximum sample depth for given PRF | $d_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$ | meters | Explains why deeper vessels need lower PRF |
| 5 | Axial resolution (B-mode) | $R_{axial} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot BW}$ | meters; BW is pulse bandwidth | Determines ability to separate two close reflectors axially |
| 6 | Lateral resolution (B-mode) | $R_{lateral} \approx \lambda \cdot f_{\#}$ | meters; $f_{\#}$ = f-number of aperture | Beam-width-limited; worsens with depth |
| 7 | Doppler angle correction factor | $\cos\theta$ | dimensionless; **0 at 90°** | **Cannot measure flow perpendicular to beam** |
| 8 | Reynolds number (flow regime) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless; $Re > 2000$ → turbulent | Distinguishes laminar vs. turbulent flow spectrally |
| 9 | Time-averaged mean velocity | $TAMV = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\, dt$ | m/s | Used in volume flow $Q = A \cdot TAMV$ |
| 10 | Volume flow rate | $Q = A_{vessel} \cdot v_{mean}$ | m³/s or mL/min | $A_{vessel} = \pi D^2 / 4$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfall at θ = 90°:** When the insonation angle is 90°, $\cos(90°) = 0$, so $f_D = 0$ regardless of how fast blood is moving. **Always maintain θ ≤ 60°** in clinical practice (and ideally **≤ 45° – 60°** for quantitative accuracy) — beyond this, the small denominator magnifies velocity errors.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vascular Surgery:** Quantifying carotid stenosis (% diameter reduction) — guides decisions on endarterectomy.
- **Cardiology:** Assessing valvular regurgitation jets and intracardiac shunts.
- **Transplant Medicine:** Monitoring renal/hepatic transplant perfusion.
- **Obstetrics:** Umbilical artery and middle cerebral artery Doppler for fetal well-being.
- **Interventional Radiology:** Guiding catheter placement, detecting pseudoaneurysms and AV fistulas.
- **Phlebology (Veins):** Detecting deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and venous insufficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Doppler Equation for Ultrasound

We derive the master equation $f_D = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ from first principles.

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry
Let a stationary transducer emit an ultrasound wave of frequency $f_0$ traveling through a medium of speed $c$. A red blood cell (RBC) moves with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ relative to the ultrasound beam axis.

### Step 2 — Frequency perceived by the moving RBC (observer)
The RBC acts as a moving **observer** of the incident wave. The frequency $f'$ perceived by the moving observer is given by the general Doppler formula:

$$
f' = f_0 \left( \frac{c - v \cos\theta}{c} \right)
$$

**Logic:** The component of RBC velocity *along* the beam axis is $v \cos\theta$. The relative speed of the wave approaching the RBC is $c - v \cos\theta$ (since the RBC moves *toward* the source, reducing the closing rate). The minus sign arises from the convention that $\theta$ is measured from the beam axis to the flow direction.

### Step 3 — Frequency of the re-emitted (scattered) wave
The RBC now acts as a **moving source** re-emitting the wave at frequency $f'$. The stationary transducer observes this re-emitted wave. The general Doppler formula for a moving source and stationary observer gives:

$$
f_D = f' \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)
$$

**Logic:** The moving source "chases" its own wavefront in the forward direction, so the effective wavelength is compressed, raising the received frequency.

### Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3

$$
f_D = f_0 \left( \frac{c - v \cos\theta}{c} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the product
Notice that the factors $(c - v\cos\theta)$ in the numerator and denominator of the two fractions *do not fully cancel* — the first has $v\cos\theta$ **subtracted** in the numerator, while the second has $v\cos\theta$ **subtracted** in the denominator. Expanding gives:

$$
f_D = f_0 \cdot \frac{c - v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta}
$$

Wait — careful. Let us re-substitute cleanly. Multiplying out:

$$
f_D = f_0 \cdot \frac{c - v\cos\theta}{c} \cdot \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta}
$$

The numerator is $c - v\cos\theta$ and the denominator is $c - v\cos\theta$, so they cancel exactly. This would give $f_D = f_0$, which is wrong. The error is in **sign convention**. We must use the more general (relativistic-style) Doppler formulation for *both* transformations:

$$
f' = f_0 \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right) \quad \text{(RBC moving toward source — observer moving toward source)}
$$

and

$$
f_D = f' \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right) \quad \text{(RBC re-emits as moving source toward stationary observer)}
$$

### Step 6 — Correct formulation (both transformations as moving-toward-source)

$$
f' = f_0 \cdot \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta}
$$

$$
f_D = f_0 \cdot \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \cdot \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} = f_0 \cdot \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)^2
$$

### Step 7 — Apply the engineering approximation $v \ll c$
Blood flow velocities in human vessels range from $\approx 0.05$ m/s (capillaries) to $\approx 5$ m/s (severe stenosis jets). The speed of sound is $c = 1540$ m/s. Therefore $v/c < 0.005$ — extremely small. Use the binomial approximation:

$$
\frac{1}{1 - x} \approx 1 + x \quad \text{for } \vert x \vert \ll 1
$$

Let $x = \dfrac{v \cos\theta}{c}$. Then:

$$
\left( \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{1}{1 - v\cos\theta / c} \right)^2 \approx (1 + v\cos\theta / c)^2 \approx 1 + \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}
$$

The $\mathcal{O}(x^2)$ term $\approx (v/c)^2 \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ is negligible.

### Step 8 — Final result

$$
f_D \approx f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c} \right) = f_0 + \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}
$$

Since $f_0$ is the original carrier (filtered out by the demodulator), the *measurable* shift is:

$$
\boxed{\,f_D = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}\,}
$$

**Engineering note:** This is the fundamental equation on which *every* duplex scanner is calibrated. The scanner measures $f_D$ via FFT, knows $f_0$ (transmit) and $c$ (assumed 1540 m/s), and with operator-entered $\theta$ solves for $v$ in real time.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A duplex scanner transmits at $f_0 = 5 \text{ MHz}$ along the centerline of the common carotid artery. The Doppler angle is set to $\theta = 60°$. The measured Doppler shift is $f_D = 3.81 \text{ kHz}$. Compute the peak blood velocity. Take $c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$.

**Solution — Step 1:** Rearrange the Doppler equation for $v$:

$$
v = \frac{c \cdot f_D}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}
$$

**Solution — Step 2:** Substitute numerical values:

$$
v = \frac{1540 \times 3810}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times \cos 60°}
$$

**Solution — Step 3:** Evaluate the denominator piece by piece:

$$
\cos 60° = 0.5
$$

$$
2 f_0 \cos\theta = 2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.5 = 5 \times 10^6
$$

**Solution — Step 4:** Final arithmetic:

$$
v = \frac{1540 \times 3810}{5 \times 10^6} = \frac{5{,}867{,}400}{5{,}000{,}000} = 1.17348 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v \approx 1.17 \text{ m/s}\,}
$$

**Clinical interpretation:** Peak systolic velocity in the common carotid artery normally ranges from $0.7$ to $1.1$ m/s. A peak of $1.17$ m/s is **borderline elevated**, suggesting possible mild (<30%) stenosis. (In routine KTU theory questions, the numerical answer suffices; clinical context is a bonus.)

---

## 3.3 Nyquist Limit Worked Example

**Problem:** For a 5 MHz transducer at $\theta = 60°$, the PRF is set to 5 kHz. Compute the maximum unambiguous (Nyquist) velocity.

**Solution — Step 1:** Use the Nyquist formula:

$$
v_{max} = \frac{c \cdot PRF}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}
$$

**Solution — Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
v_{max} = \frac{1540 \times 5000}{4 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.5}
$$

**Solution — Step 3:** Simplify denominator: $4 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.5 = 10 \times 10^6 = 10^7$

**Solution — Step 4:** Final:

$$
v_{max} = \frac{7{,}700{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} = 0.77 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Conclusion:** Velocities above $0.77$ m/s will exhibit **aliasing** (spectral wrap-around) on the display. To measure higher velocities, the sonographer must (a) increase PRF, (b) shift the baseline, (c) decrease transmit frequency, or (d) switch to CW Doppler.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Duplex Scanner

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete signal path** of a duplex scanner, showing how the system time-shares its hardware between B-mode imaging and Doppler processing. (Adapted per protocol — physical anatomical drawings replaced with functional architecture mapping.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Central Timing Controller] --> B[Transmit Beamformer]
    A --> C[Receive Beamformer]
    A --> D[Mode Multiplexer]

    B --> E[Multielement Piezoelectric Transducer Array]
    E --> C

    C --> F[Time Gain Compensation TGC Amplifier]
    F --> G[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]

    G --> H{B-Mode or Doppler Path}
    H --> I1[B-Mode Envelope Detector]
    H --> I2[Quadrature Doppler Demodulator]

    I1 --> J1[Logarithmic Compressor]
    J1 --> K1[Digital Scan Converter]
    K1 --> L1[Grayscale Display Memory]

    I2 --> J2[Wall Filter High Pass]
    J2 --> K2[FFT Spectral Analyzer]
    K2 --> L2[Spectral Display Memory]

    L1 --> M[Display Screen - Upper Half B-Mode Image]
    L2 --> N[Display Screen - Lower Half Spectral Waveform]

    I2 --> O[Audio Amplifier]
    O --> P[Loudspeaker for Sonographer]

    Q[Operator Console Keyboard] --> A
    Q --> R[Sample Volume Position Selector]
    R --> A
    Q --> S[Doppler Angle Theta Adjuster]
    S --> A
    Q --> T[PRF and Depth Controls]
    T --> A
```

## 4.2 Operational Mode State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    state1[Idle Standby Mode] --> state2[B-Mode Active]
    state2 --> state3[Spectral PW Doppler Active]
    state2 --> state4[Color Doppler Active]
    state3 --> state5[Triplex Mode B + Color + Spectral]
    state4 --> state5
    state2 --> state6[Power Doppler Mode]
    state6 --> state2
    state3 --> state2
    state5 --> state2
    state2 --> state1
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of the Doppler Signal Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[Raw RF Echo from Sample Volume] --> mix[Mixer Multiplication with Reference f0]
    mix --> Ichan[In-Phase Channel Low Pass Filter]
    mix --> Qchan[Quadrature Channel 90 deg Phase Shift then Low Pass]
    Ichan --> adder[Complex Sample I + jQ]
    Qchan --> adder
    adder --> wallfilt[Clutter Wall Filter HPF]
    wallfilt --> fftblock[FFT Processor typically 128 to 256 point]
    fftblock --> spect[Time-Velocity Spectral Display]
    fftblock --> audio[Stereo Audio Demodulator]
    audio --> spk[Speaker]
```

## 4.4 Inter-Component Interaction Summary

| Block | Physical Function | Engineering Sub-System |
|-------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Timing Controller** | Synchronizes transmit/receive switching, PRF, line-by-line scan | FPGA / ASIC sequencer |
| **Transmit Beamformer** | Applies time delays to focus transmit beam at selected depth | Analog / digital delay lines |
| **Transducer Array** | Converts electrical pulses ↔ acoustic waves; band-limited | Piezoelectric ceramic (PZT, PVDF) |
| **TGC Amplifier** | Compensates for depth-dependent attenuation | Time-variable gain amplifier |
| **Mode Multiplexer** | Routes echoes to B-mode or Doppler chain based on mode | High-speed analog switch |
| **Quadrature Demodulator** | Extracts $f_D$ from $f_0$ carrier | Mixer + 90° phase shift network |
| **Wall Filter** | Removes low-frequency clutter from vessel wall motion | High-pass IIR or polynomial filter |
| **FFT Processor** | Converts time-domain Doppler to velocity spectrum | DSP chip / GPU |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Sign of $\cos\theta$:** When flow is *away* from the transducer, the Doppler shift is *negative* (displayed below the baseline). Students often forget the directional sign in exam derivations.
> - **Factor of 2:** Forgetting that Doppler shift occurs **twice** (transmit + receive) is the single most common derivation error.
> - **Units of $f_0$:** $f_0$ must be in **Hz** (not MHz) when computing — students frequently mix units, giving answers off by $10^6$.
> - **Angle at 90°:** $\cos(90°) = 0$ → no measurable Doppler shift. Students occasionally say "perpendicular flow gives the maximum signal" — this is the opposite of the truth.
> - **Aliasing:** Students confuse *aliasing* (a sampling artifact) with *turbulence* (a flow physics phenomenon). They appear similar on screen but are unrelated.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Duplex Scanning. List its two main components and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Duplex scanning is a non-invasive diagnostic ultrasound technique that combines **real-time B-mode grayscale imaging** with **pulsed-wave Doppler flow analysis** within a single transducer and display unit. (1 mark)

Its two main components are:
1. **B-mode (Brightness mode) imaging** – provides a 2D cross-sectional anatomical image of the vessel and surrounding tissues; the operator uses this to localize the vessel and position the sample volume. (1 mark)
2. **Pulsed-Wave (PW) Doppler** – emits pulses at a defined PRF and uses range-gating to sample the Doppler shift at the operator-selected sample volume, producing a real-time spectral waveform of blood flow velocity vs. time. (1 mark)

The combination allows *anatomical* and *functional (hemodynamic)* information to be co-registered on the same screen in real time.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the Doppler equation for ultrasound. Explain why the angle θ between the beam and flow direction should be maintained between 30° and 60° in clinical practice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Doppler equation:** $f_D = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ (1 mark)

**Reason for the 30°–60° range:** (2 marks)
- At $\theta = 0°$ the beam is parallel to flow — anatomically impossible because the beam must enter the body nearly perpendicular to the skin, and a parallel beam cannot interrogate a vessel running parallel to the skin.
- At $\theta = 90°$ the beam is perpendicular to flow — $\cos(90°) = 0$, so $f_D = 0$ and no Doppler shift is detected regardless of velocity.
- For $\theta > 60°$, $\cos\theta$ becomes very small; any small error in angle estimation is **amplified** by the $1/\cos\theta$ factor in the velocity calculation, producing large errors in computed $v$.
- The range $30° \leq \theta \leq 60°$ is a clinical compromise: $\cos\theta$ is large enough to keep the shift detectable, and small errors in $\theta$ estimation translate to manageable velocity errors (≈ 10–15%).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Carotid Duplex Case Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper Adaptation]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a duplex ultrasound scanner. Clearly label the B-mode path, the Doppler path, and the display unit.

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** A duplex scanner combines B-mode anatomical imaging with PW Doppler flow analysis in a single time-shared system. The same multielement transducer array is used for both modes; the central timing controller multiplexes acquisition between them.

2. **B-mode path (3 marks):**
   - Short, broadband pulses are transmitted and focused by the **transmit beamformer**.
   - Returning echoes are amplified with **TGC (Time-Gain Compensation)** to compensate for depth attenuation.
   - The signal is **envelope-detected**, **log-compressed**, and stored in the **digital scan converter** as a 2D grayscale image displayed on the upper half of the screen.
   - The operator uses this image to identify the vessel, measure diameter, and place the **sample volume marker**.

3. **Doppler path (3 marks):**
   - Pulses at a defined **PRF** are transmitted along the same beam line.
   - Echoes from the sample volume are **quadrature-demodulated** (split into I and Q channels) to extract the Doppler shift $f_D$.
   - A **wall filter (high-pass)** removes low-frequency clutter from vessel wall motion.
   - An **FFT processor** converts the time-domain signal into a velocity spectrum displayed on the lower half of the screen; the demodulated signal is also sent to a **loudspeaker** for auditory interpretation.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A duplex scan of a patient's right common carotid artery is performed with $f_0 = 5$ MHz, $\theta = 60°$, and the spectral display shows a peak Doppler shift of $f_D = 3.81$ kHz. Calculate:
(i) the peak systolic velocity,
(ii) the Nyquist maximum velocity if PRF = 5 kHz, and
(iii) comment on whether aliasing will occur in this measurement. Take $c = 1540$ m/s.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Peak velocity:** Use $v = \dfrac{c \cdot f_D}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ (1 mark for equation)

$$
v = \frac{1540 \times 3810}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times \cos 60°} = \frac{1540 \times 3810}{5 \times 10^6} = 1.173 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(2 marks for substitution and arithmetic)}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{peak} \approx 1.17 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(ii) Nyquist limit:** Use $v_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot PRF}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}$ (1 mark)

$$
v_{max} = \frac{1540 \times 5000}{4 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.5} = \frac{7{,}700{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} = 0.77 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{max} = 0.77 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(iii) Aliasing comment (1 mark):** Since the measured peak velocity $1.17$ m/s **exceeds** the Nyquist limit $0.77$ m/s, the spectrum will exhibit **aliasing** (spectral wrap-around across the baseline). To eliminate aliasing, the sonographer should **increase the PRF**, **lower the transmit frequency**, or **reduce the Doppler angle** $\theta$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Physics and Instrumentation of Duplex*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Derive the Doppler shift equation for ultrasound from first principles, clearly stating the engineering approximation used.

**Model Solution:**
Use the derivation from Section 3.1 above, presented in 6–7 clean numbered steps, arriving at:

$$
\boxed{\,f_D = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}\,}
$$

Award marks as:
- Geometry setup and Doppler formulation for moving observer — **2 marks**
- Doppler formulation for moving source — **2 marks**
- Combination and use of the approximation $v \ll c$ — **2 marks**
- Final boxed equation — **1 mark**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the role of the following sub-systems in a duplex scanner:
(i) Time-Gain Compensation (TGC),
(ii) Quadrature demodulator,
(iii) Wall filter (clutter filter),
(iv) FFT processor.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) TGC (2 marks):** Ultrasound attenuation in tissue is approximately $0.5$ – $1.0$ dB/cm/MHz. Without compensation, deep echoes would appear darker than shallow ones of equal acoustic impedance. TGC is a **time-varying amplifier** whose gain increases with receive time (i.e., depth), so the displayed image has uniform brightness regardless of reflector depth.

**(ii) Quadrature demodulator (2 marks):** The received RF echo contains the carrier $f_0$ plus the small Doppler shift $f_D$. The demodulator multiplies the signal by a reference oscillator at $f_0$ and by a 90°-phase-shifted version, producing **In-phase (I)** and **Quadrature (Q)** baseband signals. These two channels together preserve the **sign of the Doppler shift** (i.e., direction of flow toward vs. away from the transducer), which a single-channel mixer would lose.

**(iii) Wall filter (1.5 marks):** Vessel walls and surrounding tissues move with much lower velocities (1 – 10 cm/s) than RBCs. Their Doppler shifts cluster near 0 Hz and would overwhelm the blood-flow signal. A **high-pass filter** (typically IIR with cutoff 50 – 200 Hz) removes this low-frequency "clutter" before spectral analysis.

**(iv) FFT processor (1.5 marks):** The demodulated, wall-filtered signal is a time-domain waveform containing many overlapping Doppler shifts from RBCs traveling at different velocities within the sample volume. The **Fast Fourier Transform** (typically 128 or 256 point) decomposes this signal into its frequency components, producing a real-time **velocity spectrum** displayed on the screen. The brightness of each frequency bin encodes the number of RBCs moving at that velocity.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the viva / KTU exam:**

- [ ] **Duplex = B-mode + PW Doppler** (time-shared, single transducer, single screen).
- [ ] **Triplex** = B-mode + Color + PW Doppler.
- [ ] **Master equation:** $f_D = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$; $f_0$ in Hz, $c = 1540$ m/s.
- [ ] **Doppler shift occurs twice** (transmit and receive) → the factor of 2.
- [ ] **No signal at θ = 90°** because $\cos(90°) = 0$.
- [ ] **Optimal clinical Doppler angle:** $30° \leq \theta \leq 60°$.
- [ ] **Nyquist limit:** $v_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot PRF}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}$; exceeding it causes **aliasing**.
- [ ] **Aliasing fixes:** ↑ PRF, ↓ $f_0$, ↓ $\theta$, shift baseline, or switch to **CW Doppler**.
- [ ] **Sample volume:** operator-defined region (1–15 mm) on the B-mode image from which Doppler data is acquired.
- [ ] **Wall filter** removes low-frequency tissue motion (clutter) before FFT.
- [ ] **FFT** (128 / 256 point) converts time-domain Doppler to velocity spectrum.
- [ ] **Quadrature demodulation** preserves flow direction (positive vs. negative shift).
- [ ] **Audio output** is a key clinical feature — stenosis has a high-pitched, harsh sound.
- [ ] **Major clinical applications:** Carotid stenosis grading, DVT, peripheral arterial disease, transplant perfusion, fetal umbilical artery Doppler.
- [ ] **Color Doppler** adds a 2D color overlay of mean velocity but is *not* part of classical duplex — it is the upgrade to **triplex**.
- [ ] **Continuous-Wave (CW) Doppler** has *no* range ambiguity (no aliasing) but *no* depth selectivity — useful only in CW duplex for high-velocity jets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line memory hook:** *"Duplex shows you **where the blood is going** (B-mode) and **how fast it is going** (Doppler) — at the same time, on the same screen, with the same probe."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumentation - Axial and Lateral Resolutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Instrumentation - Axial and Lateral Resolutions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In diagnostic ultrasound imaging, **Resolution** is defined as the *minimum distance between two reflectors at which they can be displayed as separate echoes on the display monitor*.
